$comment: Hand-authored golden corrections, applied by compile-qa.mjs AFTER case assembly. The vendored corpus (eval/corpus/raven-next) is a verbatim read-only snapshot (see eval/corpus/PROVENANCE.md), so eval-side golden fixes live here instead of editing the archive. OVERRIDES ARE STOP-GAPS WEARING THEIR PROVENANCE: an override corrects the eval's copy of the truth, but the defect that made it necessary lives somewhere — an upstream service gap (file it in improvements/), an eval-side authoring/rubric flaw (file a Solo todo), or plain freshness drift (say so). Every entry MUST carry `why` (what was wrong), `evidence` (live re-execution provenance — a judge's opinion alone never justifies an override), and `rootCause` (non-empty array of improvements/ paths, solo:// refs, or an explicit eval-side rationale) — compile-qa.mjs REFUSES entries missing any of the three. Entries are re-checkable: a future round that finds an entry's facts drifted updates it with fresh evidence. Mechanics: per-field replacement of the compiled case's `golden` subfields; optional validated replacement of only `tags.freshness` and `tags.freshnessHorizon`; exact, fail-loud `graderNotesReplacements` entries record inherited/effective spans and emit a separate compiled history; `graderNotesAppend` adds dated context afterward. Load-time and hand-authored — recompiles never touch this file; stale ids (not in kept cases) warn at compile time.

golden.answer:
Yes. As of **2026-07-10**, the official Stellar Ambassador Program lists **Stellar India** among its active Asia-Pacific chapters, with the status **Exploratory Chapter**. Its dedicated page names Sahitya Roy of Rise In as Chapter Lead and links current joining, events, community, and education channels.

There is also direct, dated activity beyond the directory label: 2026 operator/event records include Build Station sprints and community events in several Indian cities. One current example is the **Jaipur Builders Camp, August 14–18, 2026**, a five-day Rise In / Stellar India builder residency. Treat individual events as dated operator evidence, not proof that SDF centrally runs every activation or that participation is paid.

Regional chapters may guide eligible builders toward Instawards, but chapter support is not a funding commitment: SDF makes the final decision, typical initial Instawards are $1K–$5K in USD value paid in XLM, and $15K is the conditional aggregate maximum over time rather than an automatic first award.

q-scf-nontechnical-participation.why: The inherited answer correctly rejected guaranteed income but blurred participation with paid-program eligibility and could imply that ordinary community work qualifies for an Instaward or Public Goods award.

corroboration.claim: Legitimate nontechnical participation exists, but compensation and program eligibility are role- and program-specific
classes: A: official Ambassador, community, careers, and SCF rules
D: current SDF jobs and operator chapter pathways
F: GT-39 primary/blind independent review

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-39 primary process 3300 and blind process 3303, 2026-07-10.
Official careers and Ashby listings showed current nontechnical departments; official Instaward and Public Goods rules impose concrete eligibility and approval boundaries.
rootCause: Eval-side boundary imprecision: contribution examples were allowed to imply paid eligibility.

golden.answer:
Legitimate nontechnical participation can include community support, education, translations and content, event organization, operations, user support, product or marketing work, partnerships, design, and Ambassador/chapter activity. Current SDF or ecosystem jobs and project contracts may also include nontechnical roles when openings exist.

Participation is not guaranteed income. A contribution does not automatically qualify for a salary, grant, prize, event budget, Instaward, or Public Goods award. Instawards require a concrete eligible execution scope and final SDF approval; Public Goods has its own maintainer/steward and governance eligibility. Check the current official program, chapter, careers, or project source for availability and terms. Stellar does not provide ordinary users mining or staking rewards, guaranteed airdrops, or free XLM merely for joining.
keyFacts: Names legitimate nontechnical contribution and employment paths while keeping availability dated and source-bound.
Separates participation from compensation and from Instaward/Public Goods eligibility.
Rejects guaranteed income, mining, staking rewards, airdrops, and free-XLM claims.
avoid: Do NOT imply ordinary community or content participation automatically qualifies for an Instaward, Public Goods award, salary, or stipend.
Do NOT promise income, free XLM, mining, staking rewards, guaranteed grants, or airdrops.
Do NOT present operator-specific Ambassador opportunities as universal SDF compensation.

q-scf-nontechnical-participation.graderNotesAppend: GT-39 independently confirmed 2026-07-10. Accept broad legitimate participation examples, but require compensation and funding claims to remain tied to a current real role/program and its eligibility.

q-scf-public-goods-award.why: The inherited answer treated generic Verified membership and Soroban Governor as durable current facts. Current official detailed rules are Pilot-gated and internally inconsistent, while current maintenance/operator records describe a GitHub/Tansu transition path.

D: Public Goods maintenance working group and Tansu Q2 report

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-39 primary process 3300 and blind process 3303, 2026-07-10.

rootCause: Eval-side freshness and authority collapse: generic Verified eligibility and one platform were promoted into durable facts despite current-source conflict.

golden.answer:
The SCF Public Goods Award funds maintenance, security, documentation, and evolution of broadly useful Stellar ecosystem public goods. The durable amount is **up to $50,000 in USD value paid in XLM per proposal per quarter (roughly every three months)**.

Current detailed handbook rules are **Pilot-gated**, not open to every basic Verified Member: proposing/team and voting eligibility are tied to the Pilot tier and NQG-based governance. It is distinct from the Build Open community vote and is not a standard Build application.

As of **2026-07-10**, the operating process is source-conflicted. The handbook still describes a Soft-Launch path using Airtable and a testnet Soroban Governor, while the public maintenance working group and Q2 operator report describe GitHub intake, Pilot quorum, PG Atlas/Tansu, and a Tansu vote. State that transition rather than asserting one platform or threshold as undisputed current truth.
keyFacts: Funds ecosystem public-goods maintenance/evolution at up to $50K USD value in XLM per proposal per quarter.
Current detailed eligibility is Pilot-gated; basic Verified status alone does not grant proposing or voting eligibility.
Discloses the handbook-versus-current-operator process/platform conflict and keeps Public Goods distinct from Build Open.
avoid: Do NOT say any generic Verified Member may submit or vote.
Do NOT hard-gate Soroban Governor, Tansu, Airtable, GitHub, or one voting threshold without dated source/conflict language.
Do NOT call it an ordinary open Build application or conflate its governance with Build Open.

q-scf-public-goods-award.graderNotesAppend: GT-39 independently reconciled 2026-07-10. Gate purpose, cap, Pilot eligibility, and explicit current-source conflict; do not penalize a sourced candidate for choosing either platform only when it dates and qualifies the authority boundary.

q-scf-instawards.why: The inherited flat $15K framing hid the typical initial amount, conditional follow-ons, and the chapter-guided request/SDF-approval boundary.

corroboration.claim: Current Instaward amount and approval mechanics
classes: A/B: current official rules and pinned handbook source
F: GT-39 primary/blind plus GT-40 sibling reconciliation

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-39 processes 3300/3303 and GT-40 process 3304, 2026-07-10.
rootCause: Eval-side program-version drift and amount-basis collapse.

golden.answer: Instawards are chapter-guided, short-sprint awards for eligible early execution. A **typical initial award is $1,000–$5,000 in USD value paid in XLM**. After successful completion, a project may receive **up to two conditional follow-ons**, with **$15,000 aggregate maximum over time**; follow-ons are not automatic. There is no standard public/open application form, but there is a real chapter-guided request and scoping process. Chapter support is non-binding: **SDF alone makes the final approval and funding commitment**, generally after reviewing the scope and eligibility. Instawards are not community/NQG-voted Build grants.
keyFacts: States $1K–$5K typical initial amount, up to two conditional follow-ons, and $15K aggregate maximum.
Distinguishes no standard public form from no process and makes SDF approval final.
avoid: Do NOT call $15K the automatic initial award or imply follow-ons are guaranteed.
Do NOT say chapter leads commit SDF funds or that Instawards use NQG voting.

q-scf-instawards.graderNotesAppend: GT-39/40 atom independently confirmed 2026-07-10. Accept 'no open application' only when the answer still acknowledges the chapter-guided request/scope and final SDF review.

q-scf-nqg-voting.why: The inherited answer collapsed the four Verified tiers and distinct vote objects into a generic claim that Verified Members vote.

corroboration.claim: Current NQG tier permissions and vote scope
classes: A/B: current NQG, Verified Members, and track rules
F: GT-39 primary/blind and GT-40 Verified sibling review

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-39 processes 3300/3303 and GT-40 process 3304, 2026-07-10.
rootCause: Eval-side governance-role and vote-object collapse.

golden.answer: NQG is Stellar Community Fund's reputation/delegation governance system, combining Neural Governance with Quorum Delegation; it is not XLM/token voting and not Stellar Consensus Protocol. The current four-tier permissions matter: **Verified** has identity/account verification and view/discussion access; **Pathfinder** may participate by delegating weight; **Navigator** and **Pilot** may vote actively on Build Open projects; **Pilot** may also serve as a delegate and act on governance proposals. Build project-level community voting applies to the **Open Track only**. Integration and RFP awards are panel-led. Public Goods and quarterly resource-governance votes also use NQG-related signals but are distinct vote objects with different eligibility/processes.
keyFacts: Gives the Verified/Pathfinder/Navigator/Pilot permission matrix.
Separates Build Open voting from Integration/RFP decisions and from Public Goods/quarterly governance.
Explains NQG as reputation/delegation governance, not token voting or SCP.
avoid: Do NOT say every basic Verified member actively votes or can be a delegate.
Do NOT generalize Build Open voting to Integration/RFP or treat Public Goods as the same vote.

q-scf-nqg-voting.graderNotesAppend: GT-39/40 atom independently confirmed 2026-07-10. Grade the role matrix and vote-object boundary, not the shorthand 'Verified members vote'.

q-scf-sdf-bug-bounty.why: The inherited $250K protocol versus $50K Soroban two-tier rule is obsolete after SDF's May 2026 HackerOne consolidation and confuses runtime defects with arbitrary third-party contracts.

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-40 blind process 3304; controlling policies fetched 2026-07-10.

golden.answer: For new general SDF reports, use the **Stellar HackerOne policy**. Consolidation took effect **2026-05-07**; the old general Stellar Immunefi program is deprecated for new submissions. Under the HackerOne matrix, standard Critical/High/Medium/Low/Note maxima are **25K/15K/10K/2K/500 points**; one point is currently $1 and rewards are paid in XLM. A special core/protocol carveout can calculate a critical reward as 10% of directly affected funds, capped at **250K points**. That carveout covers `stellar-core`, `stellar-protocol`, and Soroban runtime/host code—not arbitrary third-party Soroban apps, which are outside SDF's program. The separate OpenZeppelin Stellar Contracts Immunefi bounty remains distinct, with a $25K maximum single critical payout and $250K total contest maximum. Audit Bank funds audits; it is not the vulnerability-report bounty.
keyFacts: Routes new SDF reports to current HackerOne and gives the current standard scale plus qualified core carveout.
Separates deprecated general Immunefi, the independent OpenZeppelin bounty, third-party apps, and Audit Bank.

q-scf-submission-lifecycle-deadlines.why: The inherited lifecycle omitted published durations, conditional revision, the interest-to-invitation boundary, and the distinction between selection/KYC and post-award payments.

corroboration.claim: Current Build lifecycle and stage durations

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-40 processes 3301/3304, 2026-07-10.
rootCause: Eval-side lifecycle under-specification.

golden.answer: The interest form is rolling/open; teams first undergo eligibility/referral review and only invited teams receive the full-proposal deadline. The published Build cadence is roughly six weeks: **2 weeks** for the full proposal, **1 week** for prescreen and panel review, a conditional revision step when requested, **1 week** for the Open Track community vote, then decision/notification and **1 week** for information collection/KYC. Integration and RFP decisions are panel-led and do not use the project community vote. After selection, KYC/KYB and award acceptance are distinct from delivery payments: current mechanics are four payments—10% acceptance plus three deliverable tranches of 20/30/40, generally with the next completion form due within 90 days of the prior payment. Use live round dates for a specific application.

Gives the 2/1/1/1-week stage labels and keeps post-award payments separate.

Do NOT call acceptance a fourth deliverable milestone or invent a universal results week.

q-sor-force-fast-archival-localnet.why: The inherited local-archival recipe was not operational and retained a pre-P23 restore-then-retry flow.

corroboration.claim: Current local eviction controls and P23 auto-restore flow
classes: A/B: current docs, CAP-0066, stellar-core and stellar-rpc source/tests
F: GT-42 primary/blind probes

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-42 primary 3308 and blind 3315, 2026-07-10/11.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-007-state-archival-auto-restore-ambiguity.md

golden.answer: For a deterministic unit test, use the SDK ledger/TTL test fixture. For a current standalone Core eviction test, pin the Core version and configure `TESTING_MINIMUM_PERSISTENT_ENTRY_LIFETIME` (current minimum accepted value 10), `OVERRIDE_EVICTION_PARAMS_FOR_TESTING=true`, `TESTING_EVICTION_SCAN_SIZE`, `TESTING_MAX_ENTRIES_TO_ARCHIVE`, and `TESTING_STARTING_EVICTION_SCAN_LEVEL=1`; `ARTIFICIALLY_ACCELERATE_TIME_FOR_TESTING=true` speeds ledger closes. These are Core config keys, not universal Quickstart environment variables. Avoid contract-side TTL extension, advance until `currentLedger > liveUntilLedger`, and allow the eviction scan to archive the entry. Current RPC can represent an archived entry with `liveUntilLedgerSeq: 0`; simulation of the intended P23 invoke should populate `archivedSorobanEntries`. Assemble/sign/submit that same invoke: restoration and execution are atomic. `restorePreamble` is normally nil for this path; a separate RestoreFootprint/ retry is only a manual fallback. Temporary data is deleted and cannot be restored. RPC event/history retention is unrelated.
keyFacts: Gives current version-pinned Core eviction controls and strict expiry/eviction conditions.
Uses archivedSorobanEntries and same-transaction P23 auto-restore; Temporary data is unrestorable.
avoid: Do NOT invent a lower-max/Temporary TTL flag or universal Quickstart variable.
Do NOT use restorePreamble or a mandatory restore-then-retry flow as the normal P23 signal/path.
Do NOT conflate state archival with RPC event retention.

q-sor-force-fast-archival-localnet.graderNotesAppend: GT-42 independently reconciled current Core/RPC behavior. Exact config keys are release-sensitive; semantics and same-transaction P23 path are the grading core.

q-sor-freeze-account-allowance.why: The core answer was right but omitted allowance storage/absolute-expiry and direct-contract-invoker authorization, which matter after the owner is locked.

corroboration.claim: Signer lock and surviving allowance semantics
classes: A/B: SEP-41/SAC/auth source
F: GT-42 primary/blind and host tests

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-42 processes 3308/3315.
rootCause: Eval-side authorization/storage precision.

golden.answer: Setting a classic account's master weight to zero disables that key and can irreversibly strand the account unless another signer can meet thresholds. It does not erase a previously approved SAC/SEP-41 allowance: the SAC stores the allowance as Temporary contract data through the absolute `live_until_ledger` (also called `expiration_ledger` in current docs), and `transfer_from` authenticates the spender and consumes the remaining amount. If the spender contract directly calls SAC, its own contract-invoker authorization is automatic; `authorize_as_current_contract` is for deeper calls. A locked owner cannot revoke, zero, or renew the allowance without another valid signer. Use escrow/custom token policy for a real deadline freeze; signer weight, allowance expiry, storage TTL, and business time locks are separate controls.
keyFacts: Explains master-weight lockout and survival of a bounded, absolute-ledger allowance.
Distinguishes direct contract-invoker authorization and timed escrow policy.
avoid: Do NOT call account lock a time lock for one balance or say it cancels existing allowances.
Do NOT say a locked owner can revoke/renew without another signer or require authorize_as_current_contract for a direct spender-to-SAC call.

q-sor-freeze-account-allowance.graderNotesAppend: GT-42 independently confirmed. live_until_ledger and expiration_ledger are aliases for one absolute expiry mechanism.

A classic single-unit asset can follow Draft/informational SEP-39: issue one stroop (`0.0000001`) and, if irreversible uniqueness is intended, lock the issuer after distribution. For a USDC sale, payer and NFT recipient may differ. Bind payer authorization/order intent to token, amount, recipient, and NFT parameters; an allowance alone only authorizes the spender within its scope. Validate the sale, collect payment, and mint/transfer atomically so failure rolls back. Royalties are contract/marketplace enforced, not universal. Use enumeration plus durable event indexing for contract holdings and trustline/indexer data for classic assets.

q-sor-p23-auto-restore-extendto.why: The inherited answer incorrectly required a normal separate restore/retry and treated extendTo as an absolute ledger; current P23/RPC behavior and exact formulas contradict both.

corroboration.claim: P23 same-transaction auto-restore and TTL arithmetic
classes: A/B: CAP-0066, Core/RPC source/tests, docs
F: GT-42 primary/blind

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-42 processes 3308/3315.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-007-state-archival-auto-restore-ambiguity.md

golden.answer: For an ordinary P23 invocation, simulate the intended InvokeHostFunction transaction. RPC discovers archived Persistent/Instance read-write entries and populates `archivedSorobanEntries`; assemble, sign, and submit that same invoke, which restores and executes atomically. Simulation itself does not mutate state. `restorePreamble` is normally nil in this path; a separate RestoreFootprintOp plus retry is a rare/manual size or fee-management fallback. Temporary entries cannot be restored. Restoration sets `liveUntilLedger = currentLedger + minPersistentTTL - 1` (current initial 4096 means current + 4095). `extendTo` is a relative offset: `newLiveUntilLedger = currentLedger + extendTo`, and Core requires `extendTo <= maxEntryTTL - 1` (current initial max 1,054,080 means max offset 1,054,079). Read live network config rather than freezing these numeric settings. Event/history retention is unrelated.
keyFacts: Uses archivedSorobanEntries and one submitted invoke for normal P23 restoration/execution.
States exact restore and relative extendTo formulas with max-minus-one bound.
avoid: Do NOT call extendTo an absolute ledger, require restorePreamble/separate retry for normal P23, restore Temporary data, or mix event retention into state TTL.

q-sor-p23-auto-restore-extendto.graderNotesAppend: GT-42 blind corrected the primary addendum: current normal auto-restore uses archivedSorobanEntries with restorePreamble nil.

q-sor-recurring-escrow-patterns.why: The core pattern was correct but needed payer-intent binding, idempotency, direct-invoker auth, and separate allowance/business/storage expiry domains.

corroboration.claim: Recurring pull and escrow security pattern

F: GT-42 primary/blind

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-42 processes 3308/3315.
rootCause: Eval-side security completeness.

golden.answer: Store explicit payer, token, recipient, amount, interval, next-charge, pull cap/end, cancellation/refund, and idempotency state; Soroban has no cron, so an untrusted backend submits scheduled transactions but never holds the payer key. At creation, bind payer authorization to the complete schedule/order. The subscription contract is the SEP-41/SAC spender; its direct `transfer_from` call is automatically contract-invoker-authorized, while deeper helpers require exact `authorize_as_current_contract` entries. Bound every pull from stored state, update charge state atomically with transfer, and make duplicate submissions harmless. Zero/verify/set when replacing an allowance to reduce overwrite races. Keep three clocks separate: allowance `live_until_ledger`/`expiration_ledger` is an absolute token expiry; subscription/escrow storage needs proactive TTL extension; cancellation/business expiry belongs in contract logic. The subscription contract cannot extend another contract's allowance key. Use custody and explicit release/refund conditions for a true time lock.
keyFacts: Defines an explicit, idempotent off-chain-triggered schedule with bounded payer intent.
Separates direct-invoker auth, allowance expiry, storage TTL, and business cancellation.
avoid: Do NOT give the backend the payer key or let it choose unconstrained amount/recipient.
Do NOT use TTL as cancellation or assume a contract can extend SAC allowance storage independently.

q-sor-require-auth-propagation.why: The inherited answer taught authorize_as_current_contract for the direct A→B relation, while current SDK/host rules authorize the immediate contract invoker automatically; explicit authorization begins at deeper calls.

corroboration.claim: Direct versus deeper contract authorization
classes: A/B: auth docs, SDK/host source/tests

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-42 processes 3308/3315.
rootCause: Eval-side call-depth auth inversion.

golden.answer: `require_auth` checks the exact Address passed. In user → A → B, B's `user.require_auth()` is satisfied only by the user's signed invocation tree; requiring user auth at A's entry prevents an inner authorization from being replayed through an unintended wrapper. If B instead calls `a_address.require_auth()` for immediate invoker A, the host automatically authorizes that direct A → B relationship. A must **not** put direct B in `authorize_as_current_contract` entries. Explicit current-contract authorization is for deeper calls, e.g. A → B → C where C requires A: before calling B, A authorizes the exact C invocation and nested sub-invocations. This is invocation-tree authorization, not ambient msg.sender; `__check_auth` is not used for the automatic direct-contract-invoker case.
keyFacts: Explains user auth propagation and wrapper/front-running boundary.
States direct A→B contract auth is automatic and authorize_as_current_contract describes C/deeper calls.
avoid: Do NOT include the direct next-contract call in auth_entries, infer msg.sender, or call __check_auth for the automatic direct-invoker case.

Never call `require_auth` on the account's own address inside `__check_auth`, which recurses.

golden.answer: A classic asset has a deterministic, network-specific reserved SAC address derived from `ENVELOPE_TYPE_CONTRACT_ID` plus network ID and nested `CONTRACT_ID_PREIMAGE_FROM_ASSET`; use `Asset.contractId(networkPassphrase)` or `stellar contract id asset`, never an ad-hoc `code:issuer` hash. The built-in instance is created on demand, not automatically for every reserved address. To identify a deployed SAC, read its `SCContractInstance.executable` and require current `CONTRACT_EXECUTABLE_STELLAR_ASSET`; SEP-41 methods alone prove only token compatibility. After SAC identity is established, public `name()` returns `native` or SEP-11 `CODE:G...`, and `symbol()` returns native/code, providing practical asset identity. There is no generic SEP-41 `asset()`/`issuer()` method. Do not infer issuer from `admin()`: issuer is initial admin, but `set_admin` makes current administration mutable. Deployment XDR, known-asset derivation, indexers, or implementation-level AssetInfo remain provenance alternatives; a C-address hash is not reversible.
keyFacts: Uses current network-bound derivation and executableStellarAsset inspection.
Distinguishes public SAC name identity, absent generic issuer method, and mutable admin from original issuer.
avoid: Do NOT use obsolete CONTRACT_EXECUTABLE_TOKEN/old preimage names, infer SAC from SEP-41 methods alone, claim every reserved SAC is deployed, or treat admin as immutable issuer identity.

q-sor-scval-conversion.why: The core conversion advice was correct but current SDK behavior, BytesN enforcement, union shapes, and generic-token unit terminology needed precision.

corroboration.claim: Current JS/Rust ScVal conversion behavior
classes: A/B: typed-contract docs and SDK source
F: SDK16 round-trip probes

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-43 processes 3311/3320.
rootCause: Eval-side current-SDK and unit terminology drift.

golden.answer: Prefer generated clients or `contract.Spec` conversion; Rust uses `IntoVal`/`FromVal`. Preserve wide integers as `bigint` or decimal strings—an unsafe JS Number cannot be repaired after rounding. In current JS SDK 16, `new Contract(C...).contractId()` returns the C StrKey; use `StrKey.decodeContract`/`encodeContract` for the 32 raw bytes. A `BytesN<32>` is 32 raw bytes, not ASCII C-address/hex text. Generic `nativeToScVal(Buffer)` creates `scvBytes` but cannot enforce the contract's declared BytesN length; spec-driven conversion/generated bindings do. UDT enum/union objects must match the embedded spec's tag/arm shape, not EVM ABI tuples. Token amounts are integer smallest/base units and `decimals()` controls display; “stroop” is specifically the conventional XLM/classic 10^-7 unit.
keyFacts: Uses spec-driven conversion and exact integer/StrKey/raw-byte boundaries.
Distinguishes generic scvBytes from BytesN validation, UDT shape, and base-unit display scaling.
avoid: Do NOT pass unsafe JS numbers, ASCII/hex in place of raw bytes, guess union shapes, or call every token base unit a stroop.

q-sor-sep41-transfer-vs-transferfrom.why: The inherited answer missed the current Protocol-27 direct-transfer MuxedAddress signature and needed exact allowance boundary terminology.

corroboration.claim: Current SEP-41 v0.4.1 signatures/auth/expiry
classes: B: SEP-41 and SDK27 source
F: GT-43 primary/blind

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-43 processes 3311/3320.
rootCause: Eval-side draft-interface signature drift.

golden.answer: Current SEP-41 direct `transfer(from: Address, to: MuxedAddress, amount: i128)` requires `from` authorization. `approve(from, spender, amount, live_until_ledger)`—called `expiration_ledger` in some current SDK/SAC docs—requires owner/from authorization and records an allowance through that absolute ledger sequence. `transfer_from(spender, from, to: Address, amount)` requires spender authorization, consumes the allowance, and needs no second owner signature in that call. The allowance is valid through the boundary and treated as zero when the boundary is below current ledger; a nonzero approval cannot use a past boundary. Amounts are integer base units. `live_until_ledger`/`expiration_ledger` are one mechanism, not storage TTL or seconds. SEP-41 remains Draft, so version-qualify signatures.
keyFacts: Distinguishes direct transfer MuxedAddress/from auth from transfer_from Address/spender allowance.
Explains one absolute-ledger allowance expiry under both parameter names.
avoid: Do NOT type both destinations identically, require owner auth again during transfer_from, or treat expiry as duration/storage TTL.

q-sor-stale-spec-after-upgrade.why: The inherited diagnostic falsely claimed raw JS Contract.call caches/interprets contract specs; current source and XDR probes show it is the spec-free bypass.

classes: B: JS SDK source
F: operation-XDR equality probe
A: upgrade/spec docs

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-43 processes 3311/3320.
rootCause: Eval-side SDK diagnostic inversion.

golden.answer: An in-place upgrade preserves the C-address/storage but can change active Wasm/spec. Regenerate static/generated bindings and recreate any retained spec-aware Client/Spec; current dynamic `Client.from(options)` fetches the current instance, active Wasm hash, and embedded spec when recreated. Raw JS `new Contract(id).call(method, ...exactScVals)` is already the low-level spec-free operation builder: it passes supplied ScVals to `Operation.invokeContractFunction`, and RPC simulation executes that XDR without re-encoding it from a cached spec. Use raw exact ScVals/XDR to bypass stale typed wrappers; manual InvokeHostFunction construction is unnecessary. A `Bytes(5553)` argument was encoded by the caller, not transformed by RPC. Compare actual argument/result XDR with the upgraded guest signature; `UnreachableCodeReached` is a generic guest trap, not proof of server-side stale ABI. Handle storage migration/versioning separately.
keyFacts: Identifies generated/static/retained spec clients as stale surfaces and recreated Client.from as current-spec path.
States raw Contract.call is spec-free and simulation preserves supplied XDR.
avoid: Do NOT claim raw Contract.call caches a spec, blame RPC for changing ScVals, or treat a generic guest trap as proof of stale server metadata.

golden.answer: Do not use `mock_all_auths()` to prove rejection—it makes every auth succeed. q-sor-ttl-defaults-extend.why: The durable TTL core was correct but did not answer the requested current fresh-deployment settings or give complete current APIs.

corroboration.claim: Current network TTL settings and SDK extension APIs
classes: A/B: archival docs/SDK/Core
C/F: live network settings and GT-43 probes

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-43 processes 3311/3320; live settings 2026-07-10.
rootCause: Eval-side answer completeness; improvements/stellar-docs/sd-007-state-archival-auto-restore-ambiguity.md

golden.answer: TTL is ledger-based and network-configured; reads/popularity do not automatically extend every entry. Fresh code/contract-instance entries use the network's minimum Persistent TTL. Dated 2026-07-10 settings: Mainnet min persistent 2,073,600, min temporary 17,280, max 3,110,400 ledgers; Testnet 120,960, 720, and 3,110,400. Re-query rather than freezing them. Current APIs include `instance().extend_ttl(threshold, extend_to)`, `persistent().extend_ttl(&key, threshold, extend_to)` (temporary equivalent), and deployer `extend_ttl`, `extend_ttl_for_contract_instance`, or `extend_ttl_for_code` with contract/threshold/extend_to. Instance extension covers instance/current code but threshold checks can leave different resulting TTLs; per-key calls are separate. Temporary expires permanently; Persistent/Instance archive and may auto-restore under P23 when simulation supplies archived entries—restoration is not extension. ExtendFootprintTTLOp applies one relative offset to its read-only footprint and is unrelated to account sequence bumping.
keyFacts: Gives dated network minimum/maximum settings and complete current extension method shapes.
Separates explicit extension, P23 restoration, Temporary deletion, and read-only-footprint behavior.
avoid: Do NOT freeze settings, use one-argument pseudo-code as current API, claim activity auto-extends state, or conflate restoration with extension.

q-sor-x-ray-bn254-sdk-gap.why: The premise that SDK v25 lacked BN254 wrappers is false; the inherited answer also missed the exact v25 Poseidon path and CAP-versus-shipped ABI conflict.

corroboration.claim: P25 host/SDK BN254 and Poseidon availability
classes: B: CAP/env.json/SDK source
F: pinned v23/v25/v27 compile probes

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-43 processes 3311/3320.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-021-p25-bn254-poseidon-source-links.md

golden.answer: Protocol 25 introduced CAP-0074 BN254 and CAP-0075 Poseidon/Poseidon2 host functions. The claimed v25 SDK gap is false: pinned `soroban-sdk =25.0.0` compiles `Bn254G1Affine`, `Bn254G2Affine`, `Fr`, `env.crypto().bn254().g1_add/g1_mul/pairing_check`; v23.4 lacks the surface, and current v27 uses `Bn254Fr` with deprecated `Fr` alias. For raw Poseidon in v25, enable `hazmat-crypto` and call `env.crypto_hazmat().poseidon_permutation` / `poseidon2_permutation`; higher-level hashes are in the version-matched `soroban-poseidon` crate. Host ABI remains `bn254_g1_add(BytesObject,BytesObject)`, `bn254_g1_mul(BytesObject,U256Val)`, and `bn254_multi_pairing_check(VecObject,VecObject)`, distinct from safe Rust wrappers. G1 is 64-byte big-endian X||Y; G2 is 128-byte Xc1||Xc0||Yc1||Yc0; infinity is zero and pairing vectors must be equal/nonempty. Shipped v25+ ABI uses Symbol `BN254`/`BLS12_381` for Poseidon field selection although checked-in CAP-0075 says U32Val 0/1 and contains P24/P25 tension; use shipped interface and disclose the spec conflict. This is EIP operation/encoding compatibility, not identical Ethereum calldata or a privacy product.
keyFacts: Corrects v25 wrapper availability and gives exact raw/high-level Poseidon routes.
Separates host ABI, SDK wrappers, point encodings, activation, and CAP/shipped-ABI conflict.
avoid: Do NOT claim v25 lacks wrappers, call v23 current, construct private CryptoHazmat directly, or equate Stellar API/calldata/privacy with Ethereum precompiles.

q-sor-x-ray-bn254-sdk-gap.graderNotesAppend: GT-43 pinned v25 compile proof controls; CAP-0075 text conflict must not be used to fail shipped Symbol API answers.

golden.answer: The SAC implements CAP-46-6 and SEP-41. Holder/authorized operations include balance, allowance/approve, direct `transfer` to current `MuxedAddress`, `transfer_from`, burn/burn_from, metadata, and current trustline support. Admin-only operations include mint, clawback, set_admin/admin, and set_authorized/authorized. The classic asset issuer is the **initial** SAC admin, but `set_admin` makes current administration mutable; therefore privileged calls belong to the current administrator, not permanently/only the original issuer. Each operation still enforces its address authorization and classic asset flags/trustline rules.

golden.answer: The Stellar Asset Contract is the built-in standardized-asset contract specified by **CAP-0046-06** and implements the shared **Draft SEP-41** token interface. SEP-41 is a subset/compatible interface so SAC and custom tokens can share balance/allowance/approve/transfer/transfer_from/burn/metadata calls. Current direct transfer accepts a MuxedAddress destination; transfer_from remains Address-based. SAC layers admin functions such as mint, clawback, set_admin, and set_authorized on top. The issuer is initial admin, while the current mutable administrator controls privileged calls. Every classic asset has a network-specific reserved SAC address whose instance is created on demand.

Separates shared Draft interface from SAC admin extensions and mutable administration.
avoid: Do NOT misnumber SEP-41, call it ERC-20/OZ-defined, treat Draft signatures as timeless, or call privileged methods permanently issuer-only.

golden.answer: **SEP-41** is the Draft Soroban Token Interface: the common fungible-token surface shared by the SAC and custom contracts. It defines allowance/approve, balance, burn/burn_from, metadata, current direct `transfer(from, to: MuxedAddress, amount)`, and `transfer_from(spender, from, to: Address, amount)`. Approve's absolute expiry is named `live_until_ledger` in SEP-41 and `expiration_ledger` in some SDK/SAC docs; they are one mechanism. SEP-41 is explicitly a subset of and compatible with the CAP-46-6 SAC interface, so the SAC implements it while layering admin-specific functions. Mint/clawback are standardized events rather than required generic function names. Because SEP-41 remains Draft and signatures evolved, version-qualify current code.

golden.answer: Use a typed SAC/SEP-41 token client. Current direct `transfer(from: Address, to: MuxedAddress, amount: i128)` requires `from` authorization. When the contract sends its own balance, use its contract address as `from`; the immediate contract invoker is automatically authorized, so no external user signature or `authorize_as_current_contract` entry is needed for that direct token call. Explicit current-contract authorization is only for deeper calls. For allowance-based pulls use `transfer_from(spender, from, to: Address, amount)`, where spender auth and the owner's prior bounded allowance apply. Validate amount/order state, follow checks-effects-interactions, and rely on rollback for atomic failure. Current SAC/CAP-67 transfer events append the asset topic. SEP-41 is Draft, so version-qualify MuxedAddress support.
keyFacts: Uses current direct MuxedAddress transfer/from auth and automatic direct-contract invoker rule.
Separates allowance-based transfer_from and deeper authorization.
avoid: Do NOT require ERC-20 allowance dance for every transfer, require an external signature to move the contract's own balance, or type current direct destination as plain Address.

classes: A: official Stellar Guestbook and OpenZeppelin migration pages, accessed 2026-07-11

golden.answer:
LaunchTube and Mercury/Zephyr have different roles. LaunchTube was a transaction-submission and sponsorship service: it simulated, fee-bumped or sponsored, managed a source account and sequence, submitted, and retried transactions. Mercury/Zephyr is an indexing/query layer used to discover on-chain smart-wallet state, including passkey-to-wallet mappings. OpenZeppelin replaces the submission role, not the indexing role.

Current official guidance is conflicted as of **2026-07-11**: Stellar's OpenZeppelin Relayer page calls LaunchTube discontinued and points new work to OpenZeppelin, while the still-published Guestbook prerequisites page continues to instruct users to obtain a LaunchTube JWT. Treat LaunchTube examples as legacy/path-specific and verify the maintained submitter for a new deployment; do not claim every official tutorial has already been cleaned up.

Client-side passkey authorization does not make either service a protocol requirement. An app can sign client-side and submit through its own maintained path, but fee sponsorship, reliable submission, and reverse lookup/indexing are optional infrastructure concerns it must solve if needed. Keep any relayer or indexer credentials server-side; never expose JWTs, API keys, or signing secrets in public client code.
keyFacts: Distinguishes LaunchTube's submission/sponsorship/sequence role from Mercury/Zephyr's indexing/query role.
Discloses the dated official-source conflict: the current Relayer page calls LaunchTube discontinued/replaced, while a published Guestbook page still instructs its use.
Explains that passkey signing can be client-side but relaying, sponsorship, and indexing are optional application infrastructure rather than protocol-mandated backends.
States that OpenZeppelin replaces LaunchTube's submission role, not Mercury's indexing role.
avoid: Do NOT present LaunchTube as an unqualified current greenfield recommendation or claim every official tutorial has removed it.
Do NOT conflate Mercury with Mercuryo, a relayer, or a fee sponsor.
Do NOT claim OpenZeppelin replaces Mercury/Zephyr indexing.
Do NOT claim Stellar protocol requires a particular relayer/indexer or expose service credentials in frontend code.

q-ti-multisig-recover-lobstr-vault.why: The protocol core was correct, but the inherited corpus omitted the first-party Vault recovery path and could imply that controlling the source secret always permits signer removal. GT-53 confirmed the distinct Vault phrase and current-threshold decision tree.

corroboration.claim: Vault phrase restoration and signer removal are governed by distinct product and on-ledger authorization rules
classes: A: LOBSTR Vault recovery support plus Stellar multisig/SetOptions documentation, accessed 2026-07-11
B: Android and iOS Vault recovery-key implementations, inspected 2026-07-11

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-53 primary 3372 and blind 3378; both derived current-high-threshold authority, phrase recovery, and no-backdoor semantics.
Blind child 3380 report was harvested before close and reconciled only after the independent matrix seal.
rootCause: Eval-side product-provenance omission: the golden relied on generic Stellar threshold docs without the current LOBSTR Vault recovery material.

golden.answer:
Classic Stellar multisig is account state: signer entries and weights, master-key weight, and low/medium/high thresholds. `SetOptions` changes those fields. Removing a signer means setting its weight to `0`, and the change must be authorized by the signer set that exists **before** the change, normally meeting the current high threshold.

LOBSTR Vault has its own recovery phrase, distinct from the main LOBSTR-wallet phrase. Reinstalling Vault and entering the correct Vault phrase restores the Vault signing identity. If that phrase is lost, recovery is possible only when other still-accessible authorized signers have enough aggregate weight to meet the current high threshold; that quorum can submit `SetOptions` directly to add/test a replacement and then remove the lost signer. Merely possessing a master/source secret is insufficient when its on-ledger weight is zero or below threshold.

There is no LOBSTR/SDF/network backdoor. If neither the Vault phrase nor a sufficient surviving signer set exists, recovery is cryptographically impossible. Inspect current signers and thresholds first, add and test replacement authority before removal, and never set the master weight to zero until the recovery path is proven.
keyFacts: Explains signer weights and current high-threshold authorization for SetOptions changes.
States that the LOBSTR Vault recovery phrase is distinct from the main wallet phrase and restores the Vault signer.
Explains that any valid surviving quorum meeting the current high threshold can rotate the lost signer; the LOBSTR UI is not the only protocol route.
States that source-secret possession does not override on-ledger weights and no sufficient phrase/quorum means no recovery.
avoid: Do NOT confuse the Vault recovery phrase with the main wallet phrase.
Do NOT claim any surviving source/master secret can always remove a signer regardless of its weight.
Do NOT promise LOBSTR support can bypass missing on-ledger authority.
Do NOT remove old authority or zero the master weight before a replacement threshold path is tested.

graderNotesReplacements.inherited: LOBSTR Vault product-specific phrase recovery behavior should be rechecked in Phase 3 if a primary LOBSTR support URL is required; the on-chain Stellar signer/threshold mechanics are verified from primary Stellar docs.
graderNotesReplacements.effective: GT-53 verified 2026-07-11: first-party LOBSTR support and both mobile implementations confirm the distinct Vault recovery phrase; Stellar docs/source confirm that current high-threshold signer weight controls any rotation.

q-ti-multisig-recover-lobstr-vault.graderNotesAppend: Grade the decision tree, not a product-UI-only workflow: a sufficient surviving quorum may submit SetOptions directly; without the phrase or such a quorum, there is no recovery.

GT-53 independently confirmed the product split, current capabilities, and dated release/docs/status disagreement.

rootCause: Eval-side product-identity collapse and freshness drift; the cross-owner SDF/OpenZeppelin cleanup candidate is recorded in the GT-53 audit rather than forced into one upstream finding.

golden.answer:
OpenZeppelin **Relayer** is a self-hostable AGPL relaying service. Its Stellar support includes prepared XDR submission, classic payments, Soroban invocation, fee-bump/sponsorship workflows, policies, and multiple signer backends. In self-hosted use, you configure and fund the Stellar relayer account yourself so it can pay network fees and required reserves.

OpenZeppelin **Channels** is the managed offering. It adds hosted authentication/operations and uses parallel Stellar accounts and fee handling for throughput. Hosted service billing/top-up policy is a Channels contract; it is not a generic address or rule that applies to every self-hosted Relayer. In either model, keep credentials and signing authority server-side, validate the exact transaction intent, apply app policy/rate limits, submit, and track the result.

Version and health claims must be dated. As of **2026-07-11**, the repository's latest release was **v1.6.0**, while rendered documentation still exposed 1.5.x/current paths inconsistently. The hosted endpoints returned authentication responses, which proves reachability rather than successful relaying, and the official status feed reported partial degradation/timeouts during the review. Recheck the current release, supported networks, funding terms, and status before production use.
keyFacts: Distinguishes self-hosted OpenZeppelin Relayer from managed OpenZeppelin Channels.
Describes Stellar XDR/payment/Soroban, policy/signer, and fee-bump/sponsorship support without turning managed features into protocol guarantees.
Separates self-hosted account funding from managed Channels billing/top-up policy.
Dates version/provider-health claims and discloses the v1.6.0-versus-rendered-1.5.x disagreement as of 2026-07-11.
avoid: Do NOT conflate self-hosted Relayer with managed Channels.
Do NOT publish a generic hosted top-up address or claim relaying is free of funded fee authority.
Do NOT interpret HTTP 401 as downtime or one reachable request as successful full service health.
Do NOT pin release, network support, or provider health without an as-of date.

q-ti-parse-raw-ledger-data.why: The inherited answer incorrectly treated ledger-close metadata, transaction result metadata, RPC field wrappers, and compressed data-lake objects as interchangeable raw material, and its sole source was a malformed object string. GT-53 independently confirmed exact field-to-XDR types and raw-versus-SDK/JSON behavior.

corroboration.claim: getLedgers headerXdr and metadataXdr have distinct exact XDR types and JSON mode renames the fields
classes: A: official getLedgers/getTransactions and RPC data-format documentation, accessed 2026-07-11
B: pinned JS SDK parser source and Go ingest/data-lake source
C/F: read-only Testnet RPC base64/JSON probes plus local Stellar CLI decode

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-53 primary 3372 and blind 3378; both rejected the ledger-level versus transaction-level collapse.
Blind child 3381 and parent independently recorded LedgerHeaderHistoryEntry, LedgerCloseMeta, TransactionMeta, SDK parsing, and *Json renaming before comparison.
rootCause: Eval-side source/type collapse: the inherited sources array contained a serialized rubric object rather than a URL, and the answer merged distinct RPC/XDR layers; the upstream cross-link candidate is recorded in the dated GT-53 audit.

golden.answer:
Decode according to the **source field and method**, not a generic 'raw ledger XDR' label. With default base64 XDR, RPC `getLedgers.headerXdr` is a `LedgerHeaderHistoryEntry` and `metadataXdr` is a `LedgerCloseMeta`. `getTransactions.envelopeXdr` is a `TransactionEnvelope`, `resultXdr` a `TransactionResult`, and `resultMetaXdr` a `TransactionMeta`; transaction and diagnostic event fields have their own event XDR types. `getLatestLedger.header` and `getLedgerEntries` use different wrappers again.

Raw JSON-RPC responses carry base64 strings. A public JavaScript SDK call may already parse those fields into XDR objects while retaining names such as `headerXdr`/`metadataXdr`, so check the client layer before decoding and do not double-decode. With `xdrFormat=json`, RPC renames fields to `headerJson`, `metadataJson`, and the corresponding `*Json` transaction fields; that JSON representation is explicitly less stable than canonical XDR. The CLI path is `stellar xdr decode --type <ExactType> --input single-base64`.

For `.xdr.zst` data-lake objects, decompress zstd and then follow that dataset's documented framing and XDR type; do not assume each compressed object is one bare `LedgerCloseMeta`. For production historical iteration use the current Go Ingest SDK/data-lake backend where practical. Pin a protocol-compatible SDK, preserve ledger/transaction indices, and extract operations, ledger-entry changes, and contract events from the correctly typed layer.
keyFacts: Maps getLedgers headerXdr to LedgerHeaderHistoryEntry and metadataXdr to LedgerCloseMeta.
Separates getTransactions TransactionEnvelope/TransactionResult/TransactionMeta from ledger-level metadata and from adjacent RPC method types.
Distinguishes raw base64 RPC values from already-parsed public SDK values and warns against double-decoding.
States that xdrFormat=json renames fields to *Json and is a less-stable representation.
Requires zstd decompression plus dataset-specific framing/type handling for .xdr.zst rather than assuming one bare LedgerCloseMeta per file.
avoid: Do NOT decode getLedgers headerXdr as LedgerHeader or collapse LedgerCloseMeta into transaction-level TransactionMeta.
Do NOT double-decode SDK-parsed XDR objects or expect *Xdr field names in JSON mode.
Do NOT assume nearby RPC methods use the same XDR wrapper or provider page limits are protocol constants.
Do NOT treat a compressed data-lake object as a bare XDR record without its documented framing.

q-ti-provision-wallet-per-user.why: The custody-first and account-lifecycle core was correct, but the inherited case left muxed-address semantics and reserve guidance as vague optional material. GT-53 confirmed that a muxed address is not an independent account and that operational headroom must not be presented as a protocol minimum.

corroboration.claim: Key generation, ledger-account creation, muxed routing, custody and reserve responsibility are distinct
classes: A: Stellar custody and Create Account documentation, accessed 2026-07-11
B: CAP-0027 and SEP-0023 muxed-address specifications
F: local keypair/muxed encode-decode and read-only network reserve checks recorded by the blind lane

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-53 primary 3372 and blind 3378; both retained the custody/lifecycle core and independently bounded pooled/muxed and sponsorship advice.
Sibling sweep confirmed q-ti-custodial-account-generation-c-address and q-crp-custodial-vs-noncustodial-wallets remain consistent.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-013-account-creation-merge-sponsorship-semantics.md
Eval-side completeness gap: the golden mentioned muxed accounts without defining their non-account semantics or internal-ledger requirement.

golden.answer:
Choose custody first. In a noncustodial design, the user's wallet creates or imports the key and the backend never receives the seed. In custody, choose between a separate on-ledger account per user and an omnibus account with an internal double-entry ledger plus muxed addresses; neither architecture is universally superior.

Generating a keypair does **not** create an on-ledger account. A funded `CreateAccount` operation, or a valid sponsored-reserve flow, does. For classic issued assets, add the required `ChangeTrust` trustline before receipt; SAC/SEP-41 contract-token balances are a different model. Testnet Friendbot is not Pubnet funding.

A muxed M-address is the same underlying G-account plus a uint64 identifier. It has no independent secret, signer set, sequence number, trustlines, or on-ledger balance; the base account authorizes and the application must maintain the per-user internal ledger. Numeric reserves are network settings: as of **2026-07-11**, the 0.5 XLM base reserve made two base reserves 1 XLM for an otherwise-empty ordinary account, while 2 XLM was operational headroom rather than a protocol requirement. Recheck current settings and sponsorship rules before pinning amounts. Custodial signing belongs behind a non-exportable or isolated signer with authorization, audit, rotation, recovery, and no plaintext seed exposure.
keyFacts: Starts with the custodial/noncustodial and per-user-versus-omnibus architecture decision.
Explains that keypair generation is not ledger account creation and covers Pubnet/Testnet activation plus classic ChangeTrust versus SAC flows.
Defines a muxed address as base G-account plus uint64 routing ID, not an independent account/key/balance, and requires an internal ledger.
Keeps reserve amounts dated and distinguishes protocol reserve from optional operational headroom.
avoid: Do NOT say a keypair/public key alone creates an active account or trustline.
Do NOT describe a muxed address as an independent wallet with its own secret, signers, sequence, trustlines, or balance.
Do NOT present one-account-per-user as universally superior or 2 XLM as an undated protocol minimum.
Do NOT store custodial seeds in plaintext or apply Friendbot guidance to Pubnet.

B: stellar-horizon flags source, release and Helm chart

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-53 primary 3372 and blind 3378. The blind parent plus harvested child 3379 deeply re-derived the primary helper's failed-no-output scope before authoring.

golden.answer:
Run a pinned `stellar-horizon` release against PostgreSQL and initialize/migrate the schema through the documented flow. A low-volume deployment may combine ingestion, API serving, and transaction submission; production can separate those roles, run redundant live ingesters, and scale stateless API/submission instances independently. Captive Core is the normal ingestion path. A read-only API commonly uses `INGEST=false` and `DISABLE_TX_SUB=true`; a submission-only role needs its configured Core URL. Put rate limiting outside Horizon when scaling API replicas.

Set retention explicitly. Current source as of **2026-07-11** defaults `HISTORY_RETENTION_COUNT` to **0**, meaning unlimited; official docs recommend **518400 ledgers** as an approximate one-month window for unfiltered history, but another docs sentence incorrectly calls that the default. Treat 518400 as a dated recommendation, not the executable default, and recalculate for current cadence/storage. Filters reduce retained historical scope but do not increase ingestion throughput or retroactively purge/backfill; run explicit historical ingestion when needed.

Keep `ADMIN_PORT`, `/metrics`, and profiling private. Monitor ingestion lag/timing, HTTP latency/errors, rate limits, PostgreSQL query/pool behavior, disk and process resources, and Core freshness; tune retention/filter scope, database/storage and role isolation before increasing connection pools. For Docker, options precede the image: `docker run --env-file <horizon.env> stellar/stellar-horizon:<pinned-tag>`. Use tested blue/green or hot-backup upgrades, apply migrations once on an ingest-enabled instance, verify health, then roll peers.

The canonical repository release was **v27.0.0 as of 2026-07-11**; that version is volatile and must be rechecked. The older claim that Horizon v24 'removed non-history data' remains unverifiable from the reviewed primary release evidence and is not a grading requirement.
keyFacts: Explains combined versus role-separated Horizon topology, PostgreSQL/schema lifecycle, Captive Core, and external rate limiting.
States source default HISTORY_RETENTION_COUNT=0/unlimited and distinguishes it from the dated 518400 one-month recommendation.
Explains that filtering changes storage scope but not throughput and is not retroactive backfill/purge.
Keeps ADMIN_PORT/metrics/pprof private and tunes from measured ingestion/database/API bottlenecks.
Uses valid Docker option ordering and a tested single-migration rolling/blue-green upgrade flow.
Dates any release/version claim and keeps the unverified v24 statement non-gating.
avoid: Do NOT call 518400 the executable Horizon default.
Do NOT expose ADMIN_PORT, metrics, pprof, or the database publicly.
Do NOT treat Quickstart, a floating image tag, or one hardware/connection recipe as production guidance.
Do NOT place --env-file after the Docker image or run schema migrations concurrently on every ingester.
Do NOT claim filtering speeds ingestion or retroactively rewrites history.
Do NOT assert the Horizon v24 non-history-data claim without dated primary evidence.

golden.answer:
For a full-stack Stellar smart-contract dApp, install the separate Scaffold Stellar plugin and initialize a project:

`cargo install --locked stellar-scaffold-cli`
`stellar scaffold init <project>`

`cargo binstall stellar-scaffold-cli` is the project's faster documented alternative when available. The plugin generates/coordinates Rust contracts, TypeScript clients/bindings, frontend templates, environment configuration, and build/watch workflows. Inspect the generated package scripts rather than assuming `npm start` and `npm run dev` are interchangeable. Local-network workflows require their documented Docker/local prerequisites.

For only a Rust contract workspace/sample contract, use `stellar contract init <project-path> --name <contract-name>` instead; that is not the full-stack scaffold. The current official template source included React and Svelte choices as of **2026-07-11**, and `stellar-scaffold-cli` was **0.0.25** on crates.io on that date. Both the crate version, template roster, repository redirects, and exact subcommands/scripts are volatile. Community `owner/repo[#ref]` templates are third-party code and should be reviewed/pinned before execution. `stellar-registry-cli` is optional for registry publishing/deployment, not required merely to scaffold.
keyFacts: Gives the separately installed stellar-scaffold-cli and stellar scaffold init command shape.
Defines Scaffold Stellar as full-stack contracts/clients/frontend/environment tooling and distinguishes stellar contract init as contract-only.
Dates the 0.0.25 registry and React/Svelte template examples to 2026-07-11 rather than making them permanent requirements.
Requires checking generated scripts/prerequisites and treats community templates as supply-chain inputs.
avoid: Do NOT imply stellar scaffold is bundled automatically with every core Stellar CLI install.
Do NOT equate stellar scaffold init with stellar contract init.
Do NOT freeze a crate version, template roster, repository path, or npm start/dev script without an as-of/source check.
Do NOT execute an unreviewed floating community template or omit Docker/local-network prerequisites.

q-ti-secret-key-custody-backend.why: The custody split was correct but the inherited rubric allowed vague 'KMS/Vault/HSM or envelope encryption' wording without the no-plaintext signer boundary, intent authorization, algorithm semantics, or safe on-ledger rotation. GT-53 independently confirmed those security boundaries.

corroboration.claim: Production custody requires an isolated signing authority, explicit transaction-intent policy and safe signer rotation
classes: A: Stellar custody/multisig docs plus OWASP/NIST key-management standards
B: JS SDK transaction hash/signature source
A/F: AWS/GCP Ed25519 documentation and ephemeral detached sign/verify probes, 2026-07-11

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-53 primary 3372 and blind 3378. The primary retained the core; the blind lane independently confirmed detached signing, PureEdDSA provider semantics and add-test-remove rotation.
Only ephemeral local keys and read-only provider documentation were used; no production key, paid signer, secret or transaction side effect was touched.
rootCause: Eval-side security-completeness gap; a production custody/detached-signing documentation candidate is recorded in the GT-53 audit, not manufactured as a finding without a second authoring lane.

golden.answer:
First decide whether the backend should be a custodian. In a noncustodial design, the user's wallet signs and the server never receives the S-seed. If custody is required, generate and use Stellar Ed25519 authority inside a non-exportable HSM/KMS or an isolated signer. Keep transaction construction outside that boundary where possible: validate the canonical network, source, sequence, fee, operations, amounts, destinations, timebounds and authorization intent, send the exact Stellar signature payload/transaction hash to the signer, and attach the returned signature. Never let a general web process materialize or log plaintext treasury seeds or sign arbitrary caller-supplied XDR after authentication alone.

Enforce least-privilege IAM, tenant/environment/key separation, allow-listed transaction policy, rate limits, approvals for high-risk flows, tamper-evident secret-free audit, monitoring, backup/recovery and incident runbooks. Envelope-encrypted seed decryption in a short-lived worker is a fallback that still materializes plaintext transiently; it is not equivalent to native non-exportable signing. Stellar signer weights can provide m-of-n and role separation but do not implement amount/destination limits.

Provider support is freshness-sensitive. As documented as of **2026-07-11**, standard AWS KMS Ed25519 uses PureEdDSA semantics: supply the SDK's 32-byte transaction hash as `RAW`, not `DIGEST`/Ed25519ph; GCP's `EC_SIGN_ED25519` is also PureEdDSA. Verify the exact provider/HSM algorithm and message-mode contract before production. Rotate both the external key and the on-ledger account: add and test the new signer against current thresholds before removing the old signer. Changing a KMS alias alone does not rotate Stellar account authority.
keyFacts: Separates noncustodial wallet signing from custodial backend authority.
Uses a non-exportable/isolated Ed25519 signer with explicit canonical transaction-intent validation and detached signing.
Distinguishes native non-exportable signing from an envelope-encryption fallback that transiently exposes plaintext.
States that Stellar thresholds provide quorum/role separation, not amount or destination policy.
Dates provider-specific PureEdDSA/RAW semantics and requires verification.
Requires add-test-remove on-ledger signer rotation; KMS alias rotation alone is insufficient.
avoid: Do NOT store/log plaintext seeds or let a general application process call Keypair.fromSecret on treasury keys.
Do NOT sign arbitrary caller-supplied XDR after authentication without transaction-intent policy.
Do NOT use secp256k1 or Ed25519ph/DIGEST semantics as if they were standard Stellar Ed25519.
Do NOT claim multisig thresholds enforce payment amount/destination limits.
Do NOT zero/remove old authority before the replacement signer and threshold path are tested.

rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-023-rpc-event-retention-config-drift.md
improvements/stellar-docs/sd-028-horizon-retention-default-wording-conflict.md
improvements/stellar-docs/sd-029-rpc-backfill-documentation-gap.md
Eval-side Horizon/RPC role collapse: retention, RPC BACKFILL, Horizon reingestion, Core catchup, migration/state rebuild and Galexie export were treated as one mechanism.

golden.answer:
Horizon and Stellar RPC have different retention and recovery mechanisms. As of **2026-07-11**, RPC **v27.1.1** defaults `HISTORY_RETENTION_WINDOW=120960`, `SERVE_LEDGERS_FROM_DATASTORE=false`, and `BACKFILL=false`. RPC retention is a positive ledger count; zero is invalid, and current source does not impose a seven-day maximum. An ordinary empty RPC database starts at the current History Archive State tip and accumulates forward. Increasing retention preserves more future local ledgers, transactions, and events; it does not recreate rows already pruned or never ingested.

Current RPC does have an explicit backfill path. Since v25.1, `BACKFILL=true` can synchronously materialize approximately the configured trailing window from a compatible datastore before normal serving/live ingestion. It requires `SERVE_LEDGERS_FROM_DATASTORE=true`, available datastore coverage, and compatible/gapless local state; checkpoint alignment makes exact one-ledger coverage unsafe to promise. Without materialization, direct datastore fallback remains limited to `getLedgers`—`getTransaction`, `getTransactions`, and `getEvents` read retained local data.

Horizon **v27.0.0** has a different executable default: `HISTORY_RETENTION_COUNT=0` means unlimited/no automatic history reaping. Official prose conflicts: `518400` is an approximate one-month recommendation, not the binary default. Missing Horizon history requires explicit `stellar-horizon db reingest range`; a nonzero active retention window can purge reingested rows outside that trailing window, and a requested ledger 1 normalizes to 2. Do not turn dated estimates such as 40 GB/day, 1.3 TB/month, or the v25.1 one-week backfill benchmark into universal sizing requirements—measure the chosen release, filters, schema, datastore, and workload.
keyFacts: States the three RPC v27.1.1 defaults and that RPC retention is positive-only with no source-enforced seven-day maximum.
Explains fresh-database tip start, future retention, and why raising a window alone does not restore missing rows.
Explains synchronous datastore-backed BACKFILL, its serve flag/coverage/state prerequisites, and approximate checkpoint-aligned scope.
Keeps direct datastore fallback getLedgers-only; local transaction/event methods require retained or materialized rows.
States Horizon v27.0.0 default 0/unlimited and preserves the official 0-versus-518400 wording conflict without pinning the recommendation as default.
Requires explicit Horizon range reingestion, notes nonzero reaping and ledger-1 normalization, and rejects universal storage/backfill benchmarks.
avoid: Do NOT describe retention as downloading/backfilling history or promise that a larger window restores deleted or never-ingested rows.
Do NOT use zero for unlimited RPC retention, claim seven days is a hard maximum, or use the retired transaction-retention-window setting.
Do NOT enable/describe BACKFILL without the compatible datastore, serve flag, local-state, coverage and checkpoint qualifications.
Do NOT claim a datastore extends every RPC method or infer datastore depth from a local oldestLedger field.
Do NOT call 518400 the Horizon executable default or expect reingested rows outside a nonzero trailing window to remain.
Do NOT conflate RPC BACKFILL, Horizon reingestion, Core catchup, schema migration/state rebuild, Galexie export or provider policy, and do not gate on a fixed GB/day figure.

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-54 primary process 3383 and pre-read-locked blind process 3386 independently required the MetaV4/XDR-JSON amendment on 2026-07-11.
Blind helper 3389's complete XDR row was harvested before seal; KV gt54/blind-lock/process-3386 sealed all ten rows with primaryReportRead=false before the primary/current override was read.

Sibling sweep retained GT-53's exact getLedgers/RPC JSON-mode atoms while checking raw-ledger, getTransactions, ScVal, event, HostError, signature and fee-bump cases for outer-type/passphrase conflicts.
rootCause: Eval-side protocol-release precision drift: a generic typed-XDR answer omitted the exact outer union and canonical JSON/storage contract. sd-024 and sd-003 are adjacent but not duplicates, so no new improvement finding was manufactured from golden incompleteness alone.

golden.answer:
Base64 is only transport; Stellar XDR is not self-describing. Choose the exact outer type and decode with current generated bindings, for example `xdr.TransactionEnvelope.fromXDR(value, "base64")`, `xdr.TransactionResult.fromXDR(value, "base64")`, or `xdr.TransactionMeta.fromXDR(value, "base64")`. Horizon exposes `envelope_xdr`, `result_xdr`, `result_meta_xdr` and `fee_meta_xdr`; RPC uses method-specific camelCase `*Xdr` fields in base64 mode and corresponding `*Json` names under `xdrFormat=json`. A client may already parse returned values, so inspect its types before decoding again.

Ordinary `result_meta_xdr` is the outer `TransactionMeta` union, not a bare `TransactionMetaV4`. Inspect the discriminant and call `meta.v4()` only for arm 4. Current V4 carries transaction changes before and after operations, `OperationMetaV2[]`, optional `SorobanTransactionMetaV2`, transaction events, and diagnostic events. For RPC `getLedgers`, `headerXdr` is `LedgerHeaderHistoryEntry` and `metadataXdr` is `LedgerCloseMeta`; `getLatestLedger.header` is a different `LedgerHeader` wrapper.

Raw typed decoding is network-independent. `TransactionBuilder.fromXDR`, transaction hashing and signature verification additionally require the correct network passphrase because the network hash is part of the signature base; the passphrase is not embedded in the XDR. Use `scValToNative`/`nativeToScVal` and typed `LedgerKey` helpers rather than ad hoc object parsing. `stellar xdr decode --type <Type>` is the generic CLI decoder; `stellar tx decode` is envelope-specific, and type guessing is only a shortlist.

For canonical reversible JSON, use `@stellar/stellar-xdr-json` `decode`/`encode` with the explicit XDR type. Generic `JSON.stringify` of SDK objects is not canonical XDR-JSON; preserve 64-bit integers losslessly and retain the original XDR because JSON schemas may change across protocol releases. Plain Node runtimes may require `initSync` with the package's WASM bytes when automatic initialization cannot load a `file:` URL. Prefer `@stellar/stellar-sdk` over the deprecated standalone `@stellar/stellar-base` and pin schema-compatible releases.
keyFacts: Requires an explicit outer type and gives distinct TransactionEnvelope, TransactionResult, and TransactionMeta fromXDR(base64) paths.
States ordinary metadata is the TransactionMeta union and describes arm-4 TransactionMetaV4 changes, OperationMetaV2, optional Soroban V2 metadata, transaction events, and diagnostic events.
Retains exact getLedgers LedgerHeaderHistoryEntry/LedgerCloseMeta wrappers and RPC base64 *Xdr versus JSON-mode *Json naming.
Separates network-independent structural parsing from network-passphrase-dependent transaction hash/signature verification.
Defines canonical XDR-JSON as explicit-type @stellar/stellar-xdr-json decode/encode with lossless integers and original-XDR preservation.
Distinguishes generic stellar xdr decode from envelope-specific stellar tx decode and warns about deprecated stellar-base/client double-decode.
avoid: Do NOT decode every blob as TransactionEnvelope or ordinary result metadata directly as bare TransactionMetaV4.
Do NOT treat XDR as self-describing, trust a guessed type as authoritative, or double-decode a client-parsed value.
Do NOT claim the network ID is embedded in XDR or that a wrong passphrase prevents raw structural parsing; do not hash/sign/verify with a guessed passphrase.
Do NOT decode getLedgers headerXdr as LedgerHeader or expect *Xdr names in RPC JSON mode.
Do NOT call JSON.stringify canonical XDR-JSON, coerce 64-bit values through lossy JS numbers, or persist JSON as the sole canonical record.
Do NOT recommend deprecated standalone stellar-base or claim stellar tx decode is the generic arbitrary-type decoder.

Protocol/package versions are dated evidence, not gates.

golden.answer:
For the first `getTransactions` page, send an inclusive `startLedger` and an optional `limit`. For continuation, omit `startLedger` and send the prior response's opaque `pagination.cursor`; the cursor resumes after the last returned position. `startLedger` and `cursor` are mutually exclusive. Process idempotently and persist the new cursor only after downstream writes succeed.

As of **2026-07-11**, stock Stellar RPC configuration defaults `getTransactions` to 50 records and a maximum of 200, but source exposes operator-configurable default/max options; treat provider behavior as configurable rather than a protocol constant. Horizon is separately 1–200/default 10; stock `getEvents` is 100/10000 and `getLedgers` is 50/200. Check the current provider and `getHealth` retention bounds. RPC returns failed as well as successful transactions, diagnostic events may be absent, and a cursor cannot recover history outside retention; use a suitable indexer/data-lake path when falling behind.

In default base64 mode, decode `envelopeXdr` as `TransactionEnvelope`, `resultXdr` as `TransactionResult`, `resultMetaXdr` as `TransactionMeta`, `diagnosticEventsXdr` entries as `DiagnosticEvent`, and `transactionEventsXdr` entries as `TransactionEvent`; contract-event payloads use `ContractEvent`. The public JS SDK may already parse these fields. With `xdrFormat=json`, the response uses corresponding `*Json` names, so do not expect base64-field names or double-decode SDK values.
keyFacts: States that the first page uses inclusive startLedger and continuation uses the returned opaque cursor without repeating startLedger.
States stock/configurable method limits correctly and dates provider/default claims: getTransactions 50/200, getEvents 100/10000, getLedgers 50/200, Horizon 10/200.
Explains retention/fall-behind behavior, failed-transaction inclusion, optional diagnostics, and idempotent cursor persistence.
Maps envelope/result/meta/event XDR fields to exact types and distinguishes base64 *Xdr names from JSON-mode *Json names and SDK-parsed values.
avoid: Do NOT claim the 200 limit is Horizon-only or that Stellar RPC has no 200-record cap — RPC `getTransactions` itself is capped at 200 (default 50).
Do NOT repeat startLedger with cursor, edit the opaque cursor, or treat it as a ledger number.
Do NOT present stock 200/50 as an immutable limit for every provider.
Do NOT hard-code Horizon's 200 limit as the RPC getEvents limit.
Do NOT expect *Xdr names in xdrFormat=json or double-decode SDK-parsed XDR values.

q-defi-bridge-evm-to-stellar-axelar.why: GT-15 primary and blind reviews (Solo 3243/3246, 2026-07-10) found the inherited generic checklist insufficient for the user's route question. Live quotes showed that an Axelar-branded route delivered USDC.axl while canonical Stellar USDC used CCTP, Allbridge Core, or Squid Intents depending on the provider path. Brand support is not destination-asset identity.

corroboration.claim: Current Ethereum-to-Stellar routes differ in messenger, trust model, duration, and destination USDC representation
classes: A/B: Circle CCTP, Axelar security/integration docs, Allbridge APIs, and Squid quotes
C/D: live Raven plus independent Parallel/Perplexity conflict sweeps asOf 2026-07-10

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-15 primary 3243 and blind 3246
Quote-time checks: canonical Stellar USDC via CCTP/Allbridge Core/Squid Intents; explicit Axelar route produced USDC.axl
rootCause: eval-side route abstraction conflated provider brand, messenger, and destination token representation

golden.answer:
There is no provider-independent 'safest bridge.' For Ethereum **Circle USDC → canonical Stellar USDC**, first verify a current native-burn/mint or native-liquidity route rather than assuming an Axelar label gives the same asset. On **2026-07-10**, tested routes differed:
- Allbridge Next used **Circle CCTP** and delivered canonical Stellar USDC.
- Allbridge Core used its native-liquidity/message path and also selected canonical Stellar USDC.
- Squid's canonical-USDC quote used an **Intents/RFQ** provider, not Axelar.
- An explicit Axelar quote delivered **USDC.axl**, a different contract representation—not Circle-native Stellar USDC.

CCTP burns/mints native USDC; Axelar uses a validator/gateway interchain trust model; Allbridge Core adds liquidity-pool and messaging dependencies. Re-quote at execution time, inspect the messenger and destination issuer/SAC, verify fees/limits and Circle forwarder fields, use a Stellar destination address, and send a small test. MetaMask can be the EVM source wallet, but this audit did not verify MetaMask Bridge itself as the Stellar route. None of these observations is a safety guarantee.
keyFacts: Distinguishes canonical Stellar USDC from USDC.axl or other representations and verifies the exact live route/messenger.
Compares CCTP, Axelar, Allbridge Core, and intent/RFQ trust assumptions without guaranteeing safety.
Requires a current quote, destination issuer/SAC verification, forwarder/address checks, and a small test transfer.
avoid: Do NOT claim an Axelar route currently yields Circle-native Stellar USDC without inspecting the destination token.
Do NOT claim MetaMask Bridge itself supports Stellar or recommend a route as safe without dated provider evidence.
Do NOT treat quote-time fees, duration, or availability as permanent.

graderNotesReplacements.inherited: Also good if the answer: Mentions MetaMask cannot hold Stellar-native assets directly; the user needs a Stellar wallet/address for the destination side. | Compares Axelar only with sourced evidence and labels alternative bridges such as Allbridge or CCTP accurately if used.
graderNotesReplacements.effective: Also good if the answer: Clarifies that an ordinary MetaMask EVM account/custom-network flow cannot directly receive native Stellar assets; a dated third-party Stellar Snap exists but is separate from MetaMask Bridge and is not a safety endorsement. | Compares Axelar only with sourced evidence and labels alternative bridges such as Allbridge or CCTP accurately if used.
