q-pc-multisig-setup-lifecycle.why: Core multisig lifecycle guidance is correct, but the illustrative 'three signer keys' plus a separately weighted master can accidentally describe four effective authorities rather than 2-of-3.

corroboration.claim: A safe 2-of-3 example must define exactly three effective authorities and verify replacements before master/old-signer removal.
classes: A: official multisig/SetOptions docs
B: current SetOptions threshold source/XDR

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-29 primary 3276 confirmed lifecycle mechanics; blind 3278 isolated the example-count ambiguity.
rootCause: Eval-side illustrative authority-count ambiguity.

golden.answer: Classic Stellar multisig uses account-level signer weights plus low/medium/high thresholds. `SetOptions` adds/removes signers and changes master weight/thresholds; signer, threshold, and master-weight changes require the current high-threshold authorization. For an unambiguous **2-of-3**, define exactly three effective authorities: either master + two added signers at weight 1, or three added signers at weight 1 with master weight 0 only after they are proven usable; set the relevant medium/high threshold to 2. Master weight 0 disables the account key and can permanently lock the account if alternatives cannot satisfy thresholds. Rotate by adding the replacement, verifying the new quorum/signing path, then removing the old signer while the current quorum still authorizes the transaction.
keyFacts: Uses SetOptions/current high threshold for signer/threshold/master-weight changes.
Defines exactly three effective authorities for 2-of-3 and adds/tests replacement before removal.
avoid: Do NOT set master weight 0 before alternates are proven or accidentally configure four authorities while calling it 2-of-3.
Do NOT confuse classic multisig with Soroban custom-account authentication.

q-pc-multisig-setup-lifecycle.graderNotesAppend: GT-29 PRECISION 2026-07-10: a 2-of-3 example must name exactly three effective authorities; master is one if its weight is nonzero.

q-pc-muxed-accounts.why: The G+ID/sequence semantics are correct, but the explicit where-can-I-use-it clause was unanswered and compatibility was left as an undated generic caveat.

corroboration.claim: Muxed accounts are accepted only in specified account positions; current InvokeHostFunction transactions reject muxed transaction/operation sources, and service support remains dated/conditional.
classes: A/B: pooled-account docs, CAP-0027, SEP-0023
B/F: current Core validation and CLI StrKey round-trip

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-29 blind 3278; primary 3276 independently confirmed identity/sequence/memo mechanics.
rootCause: Eval-side supported-field matrix and freshness omission.

golden.answer: A muxed `M...` address encodes an underlying `G...` Ed25519 account plus a uint64 ID. It is not a separate ledger account: the G-account owns funds, reserves, authorization, and sequence. Decode M to G + unsigned 8-byte ID; that conversion moves nothing. M differs from a transaction memo because the ID travels in a supported account field. Current protocol positions include transaction/operation source, fee-bump fee source, Payment and strict-send/strict-receive path-payment destination, AccountMerge destination, and Clawback `from`. Not every AccountID field accepts M, and current InvokeHostFunction transactions reject muxed transaction/operation sources. Wallet/exchange/anchor support changes, so verify the receiving service as of use time; otherwise use its required G+memo flow.
keyFacts: Defines M as G+uint64 with the underlying G owning sequence/funds/state.
Lists current supported positions and warns that Soroban/source and service support are not universal.
avoid: Do NOT call M an independent account or say it replaces memos everywhere.
Do NOT assume every AccountID field, InvokeHostFunction source, or service accepts M.

q-pc-muxed-accounts.graderNotesAppend: GT-29 CORRECTION 2026-07-10: grade the official supported-position matrix and current InvokeHostFunction source restriction; external service support is dated.

q-pc-practical-fee-setting.why: The bid/surge mechanics are correct, but current numeric parameters were undated and Hubble historical analytics was promoted over the direct live RPC/Horizon fee-stat endpoints.

corroboration.claim: 100 stroops is the current base-fee floor; RPC getFeeStats/stellar fees stats and Horizon /fee_stats provide current fee data, while Hubble is historical analytics.

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-29 primary 3276 and blind 3278; mechanics agreed, reviewer independently corrected endpoint priority.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-003-rpc-method-reference-pages-unindexed.md
Eval-side current-versus-historical endpoint routing error.

golden.answer: `TransactionBuilder`'s fee is a maximum inclusion-fee bid per operation, not guaranteed spend. **As of 2026-07-10**, the effective base-fee floor is 100 stroops per operation, but 100 does not guarantee inclusion under surge; a low bid can be delayed or discarded. Smart-contract transactions also pay resource fees and compete against resource limits. For live estimates, use Stellar RPC **`getFeeStats`** (also exposed by current `stellar fees stats`) for classic/Soroban inclusion-fee percentiles. Horizon `GET /fee_stats` remains available and some Horizon endpoints lack direct RPC equivalents; official Docs currently conflict between calling Horizon deprecated and calling it legacy/no-new-features, nearing EOL but not yet deprecated. Hubble is historical analytics rather than the primary real-time submission estimator. Estimate immediately before submission, cap risk, add bounds, and treat values/status as dated.
keyFacts: Explains maximum per-op bid, dated 100-stroop floor, and surge delay/exclusion.
Prioritizes live RPC getFeeStats/CLI or Horizon fee_stats and distinguishes Hubble historical analytics.
avoid: Do NOT say 100 always wins, freeze current percentiles, or use Ethereum gas/gwei framing.
Do NOT present Hubble as the primary real-time submission estimator or overbid without an application risk cap.

q-pc-tx-finality-failure-semantics.why: The inclusion/failure core is correct, but the inherited lookup paragraph incorrectly assigns PENDING to getTransaction by conflating sendTransaction admission statuses with ledger-result lookup statuses.

corroboration.claim: getTransaction returns SUCCESS, FAILED, or NOT_FOUND; PENDING belongs to sendTransaction admission/polling state, and NOT_FOUND remains retention/processing ambiguous.
classes: A: official getTransaction/sendTransaction method and lifecycle docs
C/F: live FAILED transaction RPC/Horizon lookup and provider-retention probe

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-30 primary 3277 and blind lock/report 3279; exact truth agreement on the status-surface correction.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-003-rpc-method-reference-pages-unindexed.md
Eval-side sendTransaction/getTransaction status conflation.

golden.answer: Submission is not finality. If a transaction is rejected before ledger inclusion, it consumes neither sequence nor fee. If included with `tx_failed`, the failure is ledger history, the fee and source sequence are consumed, and operation effects are rolled back according to protocol semantics. Expired or dropped pre-inclusion submissions are different. Stellar RPC **`getTransaction` returns only `SUCCESS`, `FAILED`, or `NOT_FOUND`**. `PENDING` is a `sendTransaction` admission/polling status, not a getTransaction result, and send acceptance is not ledger finality. Poll getTransaction or a sufficient-retention history source. `NOT_FOUND` alone cannot prove nonexistence because it may mean not yet recorded, absent, or outside that provider's retention. Check submission response, result XDR/code, ledger inclusion, timebounds, and retained history.
keyFacts: Distinguishes rejected/dropped pre-inclusion transactions from included success/failure and their fee/sequence effects.
Separates sendTransaction PENDING from getTransaction SUCCESS/FAILED/NOT_FOUND and explains retention ambiguity.
avoid: Do NOT say every submission is on-ledger or that included failure leaves fee/sequence untouched.
Do NOT assign PENDING to getTransaction, treat send acceptance as finality, or use one NOT_FOUND as proof of nonexistence.

graderNotesReplacements.inherited:
Verified from docs. A provider's retention window can make historical lookup ambiguous; the rubric should reward distinguishing protocol finality from API availability.
Also good if the answer: Mentions fee charging/result codes for failed included transactions where applicable.
Golden cites: At least one primary Stellar source such as developers.stellar.org, stellar-core docs, or the stellar-protocol repository.
graderNotesReplacements.effective: GT-30 CORRECTION 2026-07-10: getTransaction statuses are SUCCESS/FAILED/NOT_FOUND. PENDING is a sendTransaction admission/polling state. Included failures consume fee/sequence; pre-inclusion rejection/drop does not. NOT_FOUND remains retention/processing ambiguous.

q-protocol-cap-vs-sep.why: The inherited shorthand collapsed proposal/specification, implementation, and validator activation into one CAP event.

corroboration.claim: CAPs specify proposed core-protocol changes; implemented changes activate through network-upgrade voting, while SEPs specify ecosystem interoperability conventions.
classes: A: official SEP/CAP documentation
B: stellar-protocol core/ecosystem repository structure

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-32 primary 3281 and blind 3284.
rootCause: Eval-side proposal/implementation/activation compression.

golden.answer: A **CAP** specifies a proposed change to Stellar's core protocol. Acceptance/finality of a CAP is not itself network activation: the change must be implemented and validators later vote a network upgrade into effect. A **SEP** specifies ecosystem-facing interoperability or application conventions—such as anchor, wallet, auth, or asset workflows—and does not by itself change consensus rules. Check the proposal status, implementation/release state, and live network version separately.
keyFacts: Distinguishes core-protocol CAPs from ecosystem interoperability SEPs.
Separates CAP proposal/acceptance, implementation, and validator activation.
avoid: Do NOT say CAP acceptance immediately changes Mainnet or that a SEP is a consensus upgrade.

q-protocol-cap-vs-sep.graderNotesAppend: GT-32 CORRECTION 2026-07-10: proposal/specification, implementation, and validator activation are separate states.

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-32 primary 3281 and blind 3284.

golden.answer: For classic transactions, the inclusion-fee bid is expressed per operation and the transaction fee scales with operation count. **As of 2026-07-10**, the current minimum/effective base-fee floor is **100 stroops (0.00001 XLM) per operation**; validators can change the parameter, and surge can raise the clearing rate above it. One XLM is 10,000,000 stroops. Fee-bump lets another account raise/pay the bid. Smart-contract transactions additionally pay resource fees and compete against resource limits. Use live fee/configuration data for a current value.
keyFacts: Explains classic per-operation inclusion-fee arithmetic and dates the current 100-stroop floor.
Separates surge bids and Soroban resource fees.
avoid: Do NOT freeze 100 stroops forever, call it whole XLM/gwei, or omit operation count.

q-protocol-horizon-vs-rpc.why: The older Soroban-RPC mental model under-scoped current Stellar RPC, while official Horizon lifecycle pages now conflict on deprecated versus not-yet-deprecated wording.

corroboration.claim: RPC is the forward JSON-RPC live/current-state and contract interface but not a Horizon drop-in; Horizon remains operational for parsed/curated resources and official lifecycle wording is disputed.
classes: A: APIs/Stack/Horizon/RPC/migration pages
C/F: live Horizon and RPC endpoint probes

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-32 primary 3281 and blind 3284.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-017-horizon-lifecycle-wording-conflict.md

golden.answer: **Stellar RPC** is the forward JSON-RPC interface for live/current ledger state, simulation, contract events/transactions, and recent ledger/transaction access. It is not merely a Soroban side API, but it is also not a drop-in replacement for **Horizon**, whose REST/HAL resources remain useful for parsed accounts, payments, effects, trades, and other curated/history-oriented queries. RPC retention is method/provider bounded and many Horizon endpoints have no direct RPC equivalent. Official Docs currently conflict on whether Horizon is already 'deprecated' or is legacy/no-new-features and nearing EOL but not yet deprecated. Report that disagreement rather than choosing a categorical label; use the current migration map and endpoint requirements.
keyFacts: Explains current RPC and Horizon roles without claiming exclusive or one-for-one replacement.
Discloses the official Horizon lifecycle wording conflict and retention/endpoint gaps.
avoid: Do NOT call RPC Soroban-only, promise full historical parity, or assert a categorical Horizon lifecycle status while official pages disagree.

q-protocol-horizon-vs-rpc.graderNotesAppend: GT-32 CORRECTION 2026-07-10: encode the official Horizon lifecycle dispute; sd-017 tracks the source inconsistency.

q-protocol-ledger-close-time.why: The inherited 3–5 second cadence was stale and a single-delta primary check under-sampled current behavior.

corroboration.claim: Current official wording is about 5–7 seconds; a 199-ledger Pubnet sample had 5–7s deltas, mean 5.839s and median 6s, while CAP-0070 makes timing configurable.
classes: A/B: current stack docs and CAP-0070
C: 199-ledger live sample and timing setting

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-32 blind 3284, resolving primary under-sampling.
rootCause: Old target/marketing range and insufficient empirical sampling.

golden.answer: Stellar ledgers currently close in roughly **5–7 seconds**, not a fixed 3–5-second consensus constant. In a 199-ledger Pubnet sample on 2026-07-10, deltas were 5–7 seconds (mean 5.839, median 6). CAP-0070 made timing/configuration adjustable; a configured target is not a guarantee for every observed close. golden.answer: A Soroban transaction pays an inclusion fee plus a resource fee. The inclusion bid is non-refundable. The resource fee meters CPU instructions, ledger-entry access and read/write bytes, transaction/bandwidth size, events, return values, and state rent. Execution/I-O components are non-refundable; rent/events/return-value capacity is charged as a refundable budget before execution and unused refundable budget is returned after actual usage is known. Fees are in stroops. Use RPC simulation or `stellar tx simulate` to estimate resources and fees before signing/submission.

golden.answer: Instance storage is part of the contract-instance ledger entry and is loaded with the contract, so unbounded per-user data makes every invocation read and deserialize more bytes. Operations can eventually hit fee, resource, or ledger-entry-size limits; an over-limit write fails and the prior valid state remains, but application paths that require further growth can become unavailable—a real DoS risk, not an automatic permanent brick. Keep Instance storage small and fixed. Put per-user/unbounded data in separate Persistent keys and shard any growing collection.

Guest allocation is off by default, but the SDK's `alloc` feature or a custom allocator can enable `alloc` types at CPU/code-size cost, so heap allocation is not impossible. Contracts can read consensus ledger time and use `Env::prng`; that PRNG is public/validator-influenceable and must not be treated as secret or unbiased randomness.

golden.answer: The SAC exposes one token interface over two storage models. For a classic G address, native XLM balance is the AccountEntry balance and an issued asset balance is an i64 TrustLineEntry. For a C contract address, the SAC stores an i128 balance and authorization state in persistent ContractData. Under CAP-73, a contract can request trust for a G address only with that address's authorization and the required base reserve; for a C address the trust operation is a no-op because contract balances do not use classic trustlines.

Qualifies CAP-73 trust authorization/reserve and the C-address no-op.

Do NOT imply a contract can create a G-address trustline without that address's authorization/reserve.

golden.answer: `#[contract]` marks the Rust type used as the contract implementation attachment point. `#[contractimpl]` exports its public functions into the contract spec/dispatch surface and generates client-facing support. `#[contracttype]` makes supported structs/enums usable as contract values by generating conversions to/from `Val` and including the type in the contract specification. q-soroban-storage-types-list.why: The inherited list correctly named three SDK storage accessors but falsely called them exactly three storage durabilities, omitted the two XDR variants distinction, and underqualified P23 auto-restore and capacity.

corroboration.claim: env.storage exposes Instance, Persistent, and Temporary, while XDR ContractDataDurability has only Persistent and Temporary; Instance lives in the persistent contract-instance entry.
classes: A: current storage docs
B: CAP-0046-12, stellar-xdr, and SDK storage source
F: GT-47 CLI/source inspection

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-47 primary 3327 and blind 3334; blind independently rejected 'three durabilities'.
rootCause: Eval-side SDK/XDR layer conflation and unqualified auto-restore wording.

golden.answer: There are three **SDK storage accessors/types**: `env.storage().instance()` for small contract-instance config, `persistent()` for long-lived per-key state, and `temporary()` for short-lived/recreatable state. There are only two XDR `ContractDataDurability` values—Persistent and Temporary. Instance data is stored in the persistent contract-instance ledger entry and shares its TTL. Persistent and Instance can be archived and, under P23+, auto-restored only with the invocation read-write footprint plus `archivedSorobanEntries` metadata; Temporary is deleted at expiry. Persistent storage is not 'unlimited': entries and transactions remain subject to network/resource/size limits.
keyFacts: Names all three SDK accessors and the two XDR durability variants.
Explains Instance representation, TTL, restoration metadata, and capacity limits.
avoid: Do NOT invent ContractDataDurability::Instance or call three SDK accessors three XDR durabilities.
Do NOT call Persistent storage unlimited or auto-restore unconditional.

q-soroban-storage-types-list.graderNotesAppend: GT-47 BLIND CORRECTION 2026-07-11: accept three SDK accessors and require the two-XDR-durability distinction.

q-soroban-wasm-language.why: The inherited answer froze a 64 KiB code figure and claimed allocation was impossible. Current networks configure a 128 KiB contract-code cap, and SDK allocation is optional even though contracts remain no_std and floating point is unsupported.

corroboration.claim: Soroban contracts are generally authored in Rust/soroban-sdk and compile to wasm32v1-none no_std Wasm; alloc can be enabled, and current Mainnet/Testnet contract code cap is 131072 bytes rather than 64 KiB.
classes: A: current Rust dialect/resource docs
B: SDK alloc/build source and Core config enforcement
F: live network settings on Mainnet/Testnet

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-48 blind 3342 overturned primary 3333's no-change decision; sd-022 and sd-016 own the upstream wording/live-limit drift.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-022-no-std-allocator-wording.md
improvements/stellar-docs/sd-016-live-network-limits-stale.md

golden.answer: Stellar smart contracts execute deterministic WebAssembly. The supported/default toolchain is Rust with `soroban-sdk`, compiled to `wasm32v1-none`; Wasm is the execution format, not a claim that every source language is equally supported. Contract code is `#![no_std]`, `std` and floating point are unavailable, and host-backed SDK types are preferred. Guest allocation is off by default but the SDK `alloc` feature or a custom allocator can enable allocation at CPU/code-size cost. Do not pin 64 KiB as the code limit: as of 2026-07-11 Mainnet and Testnet configure 131072 bytes (128 KiB), and the setting is network-controlled.
keyFacts: Explains Rust/soroban-sdk to wasm32v1-none no_std execution and optional allocation.
Separates the live network-configured code cap from stale 64 KiB/data-entry values.
avoid: Do NOT say Solidity/EVM bytecode is the Soroban runtime.
Do NOT claim allocation is impossible or freeze 64 KiB as the current contract-code cap.

q-soroban-wasm-language.graderNotesAppend: GT-48 BLIND CORRECTION 2026-07-11: no_std does not prohibit alloc; current code cap is 128 KiB and must be re-read.

golden.answer: As of **2026-07-11**, Mainnet and Testnet report `contract_max_size_bytes=131072`—**128 KiB**—for uploaded contract Wasm. The separate `contract_data_entry_size_bytes=65536` value is not the code cap. These are network settings, so inspect the target network with Stellar Lab or `stellar network settings` instead of treating either number as permanent. Current Stellar CLI builds optimize by default; `--optimize` is accepted but redundant, `--optimize=false` disables it, and the standalone `stellar contract optimize` command is deprecated. Reduce dependencies/features and split logic across contracts when needed.
keyFacts: Reports the dated 128-KiB code cap and distinguishes the 64-KiB contract-data setting.

corroboration.claim: Current official x402 flow has payer wallets sign Soroban auth entries while the facilitator rebuilds/sponsors/verifies/signs/submits; the Build on Stellar hosted relayer uses an OpenZeppelin x402 plugin backed by Channels and requires its bearer API key, while API keys are not intrinsic to x402 or every facilitator.

golden.answer: In Stellar x402, the payer wallet signs the required **Soroban authorization entries**, not the facilitator's entire settlement transaction. The facilitator reconstructs the transaction with its source account, sponsors fees, verifies/simulates the authorization, signs, submits, and the client retries the paid request. As of **2026-07-11**, official docs list Freighter Browser Extension, Albedo, Hana, HOT, Klever, and OneKey; Freighter Mobile is unsupported. The hosted provider is **Build on Stellar Relayer with the OpenZeppelin x402 Plugin**, backed by OpenZeppelin Channels; that hosted setup requires a bearer API key. Do not imply that x402 itself, the payer wallet, or every facilitator intrinsically requires an API key. Coinbase remains Testnet-only in the official roster; use any compatible SEP-41 token, with USDC the default example.
keyFacts: Separates payer auth-entry signing from facilitator transaction sponsorship/submission.

avoid: Do NOT say the payer signs the facilitator's full settlement transaction.

golden.answer: Yes on current networks. **CAP-0074** and **CAP-0075** are Final and shipped in Protocol 25: BN254 G1 add/mul and multi-pairing checks, plus configurable Poseidon/Poseidon2 permutation host functions over supported scalar fields. **CAP-0080 / Protocol 26** added efficient BN254 MSM, scalar arithmetic, and curve checks. Mainnet/Testnet now report Protocol 27, and soroban-sdk 27 exposes BN254 plus Poseidon/Poseidon2 APIs. These are cryptographic primitives, not turnkey UltraHonk/Groth16 verifiers, fixed application hashes, or end-to-end private-payment systems; match the proof curve, encoding, parameters, and application policy.
keyFacts: Names CAP-74/75 as Final/P25 and CAP-80 as the P26 extension.

avoid: Do NOT call BN254/Poseidon merely proposed or swap CAP-74/75 roles.

golden.answer: Concrete examples exist, but they are not interchangeable or production endorsements. The official `stellar/soroban-examples/groth16_verifier` is a **BLS12-381** demonstration that computes the verification-key linear combination and calls `pairing_check`; the repo also has a BLS12-381 privacy-pools proof of concept. `NethermindEth/stellar-risc0-verifier` contains a separate **BN254/RISC Zero Groth16** verifier using newer primitives. Both are described as demonstration/unaudited code. Production contracts must pin/control the verifying key, validate public-input meaning and lengths, bind proof acceptance to the intended action/context and replay/nullifier state, and match the exact curve/proof encoding. Proof validity alone does not prove application intent or prevent replay.
keyFacts: Names the official BLS12-381 and Nethermind BN254 Groth16 examples and their distinct systems.
Requires unaudited/demo caveats, verifying-key control, input semantics, context and replay binding.
avoid: Do NOT say no examples exist or call the official example BN254.
Do NOT claim examples are audited/production-safe or that a valid proof alone prevents replay/proves business intent.

Peer transport is separate in `Stellar-overlay.x`: `GET_SCP_QUORUMSET` requests by quorum-set hash, `SCP_QUORUMSET` carries the set, `SCP_MESSAGE` carries `SCPEnvelope`, and `GET_SCP_STATE` requests state by ledger sequence. q-infra-query-contract-events-rpc-howto.why: The inherited how-to admitted the removed diagnostic event type, flattened filter/topic semantics, and pinned stale 24-hour/seven-day retention wording while conflating event page size, ledger scan span, and retained history.

corroboration.claim: Current getEvents accepts optional system/contract filters, cursor pagination, XDR topic matchers, and bounded provider-configured history; diagnostic is rejected.
classes: A: current getEvents and event-ingestion documentation
B: current stellar-docs OpenRPC plus stellar-rpc v27.1.1 source/release
F: read-only SDF getEvents/getHealth and diagnostic-type rejection probes

corroboration.claim: Page size, per-request ledger scan span, and retained ledger history are three separate limits.
classes: B: stellar-rpc get_events.go and config/options.go
A: OpenRPC pagination schema and RPC configuration guide

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-50 primary process 3349 and blind process 3361 independently derived the correction; blind matrix sealed at 2026-07-11T03:53:28.522Z before the primary read.

Sibling sweep checked q-soroban-publish-events, q-soroban-event-indexing-design, q-ti-rpc-gettransactions-pagination-xdr, and q-ti-historical-events-beyond-retention; the latter is corrected in the same GT-52 bundle.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-023-rpc-event-retention-config-drift.md
improvements/stellar-docs/sd-004-rpc-limits-described-as-hardcoded.md
Eval-side API-schema drift: the archived answer preserved a removed event type and incomplete cursor/filter semantics.

golden.answer:
Call Stellar RPC **`getEvents`** (or its SDK wrapper). Supply optional `startLedger` (inclusive), `endLedger` (exclusive), and up to five filters. External `type`, when present, is **`system` or `contract`**; current RPC rejects `diagnostic`. Filter objects are alternatives, populated constraints within a filter are conjunctive, contract IDs are capped per filter, and topic matchers are 1–4 base64-encoded `ScVal` segments with wildcard semantics defined by the method schema.

Page with the returned cursor; when resuming from a cursor, omit explicit ledger bounds. Persist a cursor only after downstream success and deduplicate by event ID. Stock response sizing is 100 events by default and 10,000 maximum but operator-configurable; the current handler also limits one request's ledger scan span to 10,000 ledgers. Neither number is retention. Retention is the endpoint's configured recent ledger window, so inspect `getHealth`/provider docs and continuously ingest or use an indexer/Hubble for older history. Horizon does not serve contract events, and there is no `getContractEvents` method.
keyFacts: Uses Stellar RPC getEvents; current stream types are system and contract, not diagnostic.
Uses inclusive/exclusive bounds or a returned cursor, persists progress after successful processing, and deduplicates by event ID.
Keeps response-event page size, request ledger scan span, and provider-configured retention separate.
Routes durable history to continuous ingestion, an indexer, or Hubble rather than Horizon or an invented RPC method.
avoid: Do NOT request `diagnostic` through getEvents; diagnostic output belongs to transaction/simulation diagnostics.
Do NOT combine a resume cursor with explicit start/end ledger bounds.
Do NOT present topic segments as plain text without the ScVal/XDR qualification.
Do NOT normalize retention to a universal 24-hour default or seven-day maximum, or conflate it with page size/scan span.
Do NOT use Horizon or invent `getContractEvents`.

q-infra-query-contract-events-rpc-howto.graderNotesAppend: GT-50 CORRECTION 2026-07-11: require current system/contract event types, cursor/idempotence, and the three-limit distinction. The official retention prose conflict is tracked in sd-023; provider/configuration-aware answers control.

q-infra-quickstart-local-network.why: The inherited answer omitted Futurenet, used the stale `stellar network container start` command, and blurred hermetic local mode with Quickstart modes that connect to public networks and proxy their public faucets.

corroboration.claim: Current Stellar CLI uses `stellar container start`, and Quickstart supports local, testnet, futurenet, and pubnet.

golden.answer:
For a disposable local development or CI network, run **`stellar container start local`**. The direct Docker equivalent is `docker run -i -p 8000:8000 stellar/quickstart --local`. Quickstart bundles Core, Horizon, Stellar RPC, Friendbot, and Lab; the canonical local RPC path is `http://localhost:8000/rpc`.

Quickstart supports four modes: **local, testnet, futurenet, and pubnet**. Only `local` is a hermetic standalone network with a local faucet. Testnet/Futurenet/Pubnet modes connect to those public networks; Testnet/Futurenet Friendbot access is proxied and does not evade the public faucet's limits. Quickstart is development infrastructure, not a production deployment, and its state is disposable unless persistence is explicitly configured.
keyFacts: Uses the current `stellar container start local` command or the `stellar/quickstart --local` Docker image.
Names all four modes: local, testnet, futurenet, and pubnet.
States that only local mode is hermetic; public-network modes do not create independent public faucets.
Treats Quickstart as disposable development/CI infrastructure, not production.
avoid: Do NOT use `stellar network container start` as the current canonical command or omit Futurenet from an exhaustive mode list.
Do NOT claim Testnet/Futurenet/Pubnet modes are hermetic or bypass public faucet limits.
Do NOT recommend Quickstart as a production node or promise persistence without configuration.

golden.answer:
There is no provider-independent number of days. Stellar RPC **`getEvents`** reads only the endpoint's configured recent ledger window. Current stock source and the public endpoints probed on **2026-07-11** used **120,960 ledgers** (roughly seven days at typical close times), but operators may configure a different positive window. Inspect `getHealth.oldestLedger`, `latestLedger`, and `ledgerRetentionWindow`, plus provider documentation, and handle range errors.

The generated getEvents page still contains both a 24-hour-default statement and a seven-day-maximum statement; those conflict with current source/admin/live behavior and are not universal grading gates. For older events, continuously ingest and deduplicate them or use a historical indexer/Hubble. RPC Archive/data-lake integration extends **`getLedgers` only**; it does not backfill historical `getEvents`.

Uses 120,960 ledgers only as a dated current stock/live observation, not a universal maximum.
Uses continuous ingestion, an indexer, or Hubble for older events and keeps archive getLedgers separate.
avoid: Do NOT pin a universal 24-hour default or seven-day maximum.
Do NOT claim RPC retains full event history.
Do NOT infer historical getEvents coverage from an RPC Archive/getLedgers capability.

golden.answer:

`getLedgers` exists on ordinary RPC instances within their retained range; deep/full ledger history requires an archive/data-lake-backed provider. `getEvents` is likewise bounded by provider-configured retention. SDK helpers such as `getAccount`, Horizon REST paths, removed singular `getLedgerEntry`, and disabled `rpc.*` discovery methods are not additional application methods.

q-infra-secp256r1-passkeys.why: The inherited answer universalized P-256 across WebAuthn and collapsed a full WebAuthn assertion/policy check into CAP-0051's low-level signature primitive, creating a security-sensitive false equivalence.

corroboration.claim: CAP-0051 is Final/Protocol 21 and supplies a low-level secp256r1 verification host function, not full WebAuthn parsing or authorization policy.
classes: B: CAP-0051 and host/SDK interfaces
A: current smart-wallet/contract-auth documentation

corroboration.claim: P-256/ES256 is common for WebAuthn but not universal; origin, RP ID, flags, credential, replay/counter, and authorization-context checks remain separate.
classes: A: W3C WebAuthn Level 3
B: OpenZeppelin stellar-contracts v0.7.2 WebAuthn verifier source
F: GT-50 independent security-layer reconciliation

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-50 primary process 3349 confirmed the most-versus-all correction; blind process 3361 independently required the fuller WebAuthn/host/__check_auth separation.
Sibling sweep checked q-protocol-passkeys-secp256r1, q-soroban-check-auth-custom-account, q-tool-passkeykit-smart-wallet, q-tool-passkey-wallet-recovery, and q-soroban-add-signer-smart-wallet-howto.
rootCause: Eval-side security-layer collapse: WebAuthn ceremony, signature primitive, and contract authorization policy were authored as one operation.

golden.answer:
A Stellar passkey smart account combines three layers: the browser's **WebAuthn ceremony**, the contract account's **`__check_auth` authorization policy**, and **CAP-0051's Protocol-21 secp256r1/P-256 signature-verification host primitive**. The host verifies a 65-byte uncompressed SEC1 public key, a 32-byte digest, and a 64-byte compact low-s signature; it does **not** parse or semantically validate a WebAuthn assertion.

Most/common WebAuthn credentials using COSE ES256 use P-256, but WebAuthn also permits other algorithms. The relying party/verifier must bind the challenge and intended authorization payload, parse `clientDataJSON`/`authenticatorData`, and enforce the chosen origin, RP-ID hash, user-presence/verification and backup flags, credential identity, expiry/replay/counter policy, and application authorization context. A reviewed OpenZeppelin verifier supplies part of that validation and intentionally leaves several relying-party checks to application policy. A separate classic account/relayer submits and pays; fee sponsorship does not replace smart-account authorization.
keyFacts: CAP-0051/P21 supplies the native P-256 verification primitive used inside contract-account authorization.
P-256/ES256 is common for WebAuthn, not the universal passkey curve.
Full WebAuthn and authorization require challenge/context, origin/RP-ID, flags, credential, replay/counter, and policy checks beyond a valid P-256 signature.
Separates the authorizing C-account from the G-account/relayer that submits and pays.
avoid: Do NOT claim CAP-0051 performs full WebAuthn verification or that a valid P-256 signature alone authorizes an operation.
Do NOT claim every passkey/WebAuthn credential uses P-256.
Do NOT claim origin, RP-ID, flags, counters, or replay are automatically enforced by the host primitive.
Do NOT confuse P-256 with secp256k1, Classic Ed25519 signer semantics, or fee sponsorship.

q-infra-secp256r1-passkeys.graderNotesAppend: GT-50 SECURITY CORRECTION 2026-07-11: grade the layered WebAuthn ceremony → application/RP validation → __check_auth policy → CAP-0051 primitive model. The versioned OZ example is evidence of deliberate policy boundaries, not a universal verifier contract.

Friendbot funds **accounts** on the applicable test network; it does not directly fund a contract. Provider coverage and live protocol versions change, so consult the current Networks/Providers pages and date any exact snapshot rather than using it to define the networks forever.

B: stellar-cli v27 template/generated source and JS SDK/Freighter releases

corroboration.claim: Tx.fee includes the inclusion bid plus sorobanData.resourceFee, whose budget includes refundable and non-refundable components.

golden.answer:
Generate TypeScript bindings with the current **`stellar contract bindings typescript`** command, then consume the generated output as a workspace/local, packed, or published npm package. Inspect the generated package itself: current v27 output exports a typed **`Client`** and network metadata, while generated README snippets can lag. In a Next.js App Router app, keep Freighter in a **`"use client"`** component. Obtain the public address/permission, verify the expected network passphrase, create the generated client with its contract/network/RPC/signing options, call the typed method, let simulation/assembly provide Soroban data/auth/resources, sign the assembled XDR with Freighter, submit through RPC, and poll. Never put a secret key in the browser.

For a contract transaction, `Tx.fee` is the maximum total: the inclusion-fee bid plus `sorobanData.resourceFee`. The resource budget includes non-refundable charges and potentially refundable budget; do not call the whole resource fee automatically refundable or the total fee inclusion-only.
keyFacts: Uses the current stellar contract bindings typescript command and generated Client/types rather than stale README names.
Uses a workspace/installed package and a Next.js client boundary for Freighter, with explicit network validation and no browser secret.

q-ti-classic-submission-errors.why: The inherited taxonomy mixed generic and operation-specific result-code namespaces, omitted named Payment/SetOptions mappings, and did not make transport uncertainty versus deterministic rejection sufficiently explicit.

corroboration.claim: Underfunding is spendable-balance/reserve/liability dependent, and timeout/429/5xx uncertainty requires hash-first handling rather than blind rebuilds.
classes: A: error handling and offer/payment docs
B: Classic transaction atomicity/result semantics

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-51 primary/blind matrices independently required the taxonomy repair; blind corrected the primary's overbroad treatment of `op_bad_signer`.

golden.answer:
Start with HTTP status and Horizon `extras.result_codes.transaction` plus `operations`. `tx_bad_seq` is a source-sequence mismatch; `tx_failed` means an operation failed and the transaction stayed atomic, so inspect the operation array. Underfunded means **spendable** balance after minimum reserve, subentries, offers, and selling liabilities—not merely the displayed nominal balance.

Use the exact operation family and namespace. Payment mappings include `PAYMENT_NO_DESTINATION` and `PAYMENT_SRC_NO_TRUST`; SetOptions documents `op_bad_signer` paired with `SET_OPTIONS_BAD_SIGNER`; generic operation authorization is `op_bad_auth`. These labels are not interchangeable. Treat deterministic 400/result-code failures differently from 429/5xx/timeouts: back off for transport/admission uncertainty and query the known hash before rebuilding, preserving idempotency and bounds.
keyFacts: Reads transaction and operation result codes and preserves transaction atomicity.
Explains spendable-balance effects from reserves, subentries, offers, liabilities, and trustlines.
Correctly scopes PAYMENT_NO_DESTINATION/PAYMENT_SRC_NO_TRUST, SetOptions op_bad_signer/SET_OPTIONS_BAD_SIGNER, and generic op_bad_auth.
Uses hash-first/idempotent handling for 429/5xx/timeouts instead of retrying every 400.
avoid: Do NOT retry every HTTP 400 or call a timeout/polling miss definitive failure.
Do NOT ignore operation-level codes or mix generic, Payment, and SetOptions namespaces.
Do NOT diagnose all underfunding as a faucet/provider issue or advise bypassing reserves/trustlines.

q-ti-classic-submission-errors.graderNotesAppend: GT-51 CORRECTION 2026-07-11: `op_bad_signer` is valid only as the documented SetOptions-specific label; do not genericize or delete it.

q-ti-contract-verification-explorers.why: The inherited answer blurred metadata, attested builds, and independent reproducibility; overstated unverified explorer badge support; and allowed wallet listing to look like a consequence of deployment or verification.

corroboration.claim: SEP-55 and SEP-58 are Draft complementary build-provenance/reproducibility standards; current Stellar Lab implements attestation-based Build Info verification.
classes: A: normative SEP-55/SEP-58 and Lab docs
B: current Lab getBuildVerification implementation

corroboration.claim: Build Verified proves matching build provenance, not safety/audit/correctness or universal explorer/wallet support.
classes: A: SEP security/trust-model disclaimers
B: Lab implementation and current explorer-source sweep

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-51 primary/blind matrices independently required a material verification/product-policy repair; blind independently disproved a research synthesis that SEP-58 did not exist.
Sibling sweep checked q-soroban-contract-build-verification, which already encodes the SEP-55/58 complementary model.
rootCause: Eval-side authority collapse: interface metadata, attestation, reproducible build, explorer badges, and wallet listing were treated as one verification state.

golden.answer:
Separate the layers. **SEP-46** metadata and **SEP-48** interface/spec availability do not prove source. **SEP-55** (Draft) defines GitHub-attestation-based build verification; current Stellar Lab Contract Explorer is the directly confirmed implementation. A Lab **Build Verified** result means the attested workflow produced Wasm matching the deployed hash/repository evidence. It does not prove the source is safe, audited, correct, independently reproducible, or free of malicious build steps. **SEP-58** is a separate Draft standard for describing a digest-pinned environment/options/source so another builder can compare bytes.

`release.yml` is a GitHub workflow convention used to publish build artifacts/attestations, not protocol state and not proof by itself. Do not assume StellarExpert, StellarChain, or every explorer exposes the same badge without current direct evidence. Wallet visibility is separate: LOBSTR's classic-asset listing policy is not a universal custom-token policy, and SAC/custom SEP-41 visibility remains wallet/indexer-specific. q-ti-custodial-account-generation-c-addr.why: The inherited G/C split was correct but omitted keypair-versus-ledger-account creation, sponsored creation, contract-defined C authorization/fee sponsorship, and the concrete muxed-address route for custodial memo deposits.

corroboration.claim: A custodial G account is an Ed25519 keypair plus an on-ledger account; a C address is a contract ID with contract-defined authorization and no corresponding Stellar seed.
classes: A: account creation, contract-account, SAC, and C-account transfer docs
B: current JS SDK Keypair/StrKey source
F: key/address validation and contract-ID derivation without secret output/deployment

corroboration.claim: C-to-custodian memo flows use a supplied M address or G plus u64 muxed ID; provider/exchange C-address support remains dated.
classes: A: current C-account transfer/muxed-address guidance
B: CAP-67/current event fields

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-51 primary/blind matrices independently agreed on memo/muxed and freshness repair; blind added keypair/live-account and C authorization boundaries.
Sibling sweep checked q-sor-contract-trustlines-c-address, q-sep-45-contract-auth, and account-creation siblings; sd-013 already owns sponsored-creation docs semantics.
rootCause: improvements/stellar-docs/sd-013-account-creation-merge-sponsorship-semantics.md
Eval-side custody/contract-account completeness gap.

golden.answer:
For a conventional custodial account, generate an Ed25519 keypair only in the backend/HSM/KMS, expose the `G...` public address, and keep the `S...` secret out of clients/logs. `Keypair.random()` creates key material, not an on-ledger account; create/fund the account with `CreateAccount` or a documented sponsored-reserve flow. Vanity G addresses require brute-force key generation, so outsourcing exposes the winning secret.

A `C...` address is a contract ID, not a keypair and not creatable with `CreateAccount`. A C account has no corresponding Stellar seed and authorizes Soroban auth entries through its contract-defined `__check_auth`; a separate G account submits and pays/sponsors fees. G-to-C asset funding is a simulated/assembled SAC/token `transfer`, and C balances are relevant token/SAC contract state. If an exchange does not currently support C withdrawals, withdraw to a controlled G account and transfer to C. For C-to-custodian deposits requiring a memo ID, use the custodian's supplied `M...` address or construct the muxed destination from G plus the u64 ID; keep provider support dated.
keyFacts: Separates generated G/S key material from creation/funding of an on-ledger account and requires backend custody.
States that C is a contract ID with contract-defined auth, no seed, and a separate fee-paying/submitting G account.
Uses SAC/token transfer and contract-state balance semantics for G↔C.
Uses muxed M/G+u64 handling for memo-based custodial deposits and dates exchange support.
avoid: Do NOT generate/log custodial secrets in clients or accept third-party vanity seeds.
Do NOT call a C address a keypair/classic account, use CreateAccount/Classic Payment to C, or equate ID prediction with deployment.
Do NOT claim an exchange supports C addresses without a current provider source or omit muxed memo handling.

For a practical current list, Stellar Lab currently consumes a paginated StellarExpert contract endpoint; treat that as third-party indexed coverage, not a protocol registry or completeness guarantee. For ledger-controlled coverage, use Hubble/current state views or ingest complete ledger metadata with Galexie/Ingest SDK, identify contract-instance ledger entries, join TTL/current state, and replay creation/archive history when the requirement is all-ever. Use the live Hubble catalog names at query time rather than freezing guessed SQL. classes: A: current Horizon accounts/pagination and SAC/Hubble docs
C: live `/accounts?asset` cursor response with a zero-balance trustline

golden.answer:
For Classic `G...` trustlines, paginate Horizon **`GET /accounts?asset=CODE:ISSUER`** and follow the server-provided next link/cursor; do not build a giant account-ID URL. The collection represents trustline accounts and can include a zero balance, so inspect the target balance row and apply the campaign's positive-balance/authorization rules. It does not include `C...` contract balances.

A multi-page live crawl is not an atomic exact-ledger snapshot. For deterministic distribution, pin the network and target ledger and use Hubble/owned ingestion or another provider that explicitly supplies snapshot semantics. Define treatment of claimable balances, liquidity pools, frozen/unauthorized trustlines, issuer/distribution accounts, muxed IDs, and contract holders. For SAC/custom SEP-41 holders, use complete indexed state/events plus current-balance validation; SEP-41 does not standardize storage enumeration. Freeze a recipient ledger, store computed amount/tx hash/status/retry count, manage sequence/fees, dry-run/canary, and make retries idempotent.
keyFacts: Uses cursor-paginated `/accounts?asset=CODE:ISSUER` for Classic trustlines and filters zero balances/eligibility.
States that the Horizon collection excludes C-address token balances and a moving crawl is not an atomic historical snapshot.
Pins network/ledger and defines claimable/pool/auth/issuer/muxed/contract eligibility.
Requires an idempotent recipient ledger and retry/sequence/fee/canary controls.
avoid: Do NOT equate trustline existence with a positive holder or claim `/accounts?asset` includes contract holders.
Do NOT call a moving Horizon crawl an exact-ledger snapshot or stuff hundreds of IDs into one URL.
Do NOT airdrop before defining holder classes/deduplication or without idempotent retry state.

q-ti-fetch-all-balances-classic-sac.why: The inherited storage split was correct but omitted the no-double-count rule for a G account's Classic asset through SAC, the candidate-token discovery boundary, arbitrary SEP-41 storage limits, and balance provenance/units.

corroboration.claim: Horizon account balances cover native XLM and Classic trustlines/LP shares; a G-account SAC view uses the same underlying Classic balance and must not be counted twice.
classes: A: Horizon account and SAC accounting documentation
C: live Horizon asset/account aggregate checks

corroboration.claim: C-address/known-token balances are contract state/interface reads, but no standard one-call registry discovers every SAC/custom SEP-41 token and arbitrary storage is not standardized.
classes: A: SAC, getLedgerEntries, Hubble, and token docs

golden.answer:
Use separate discovery/accounting paths. For a `G...` account, Horizon `/accounts/{G}` returns native XLM plus Classic trustline and LP-share rows. When that G account uses a Classic asset through its SAC interface, it is the **same underlying account/trustline balance**, not a second wrapped copy—do not double-count it.

For a `C...` address, SAC/custom-token balances are contract state. For a **known** token, call its SEP-41 `balance(address)` interface or use the corresponding ledger/indexed state. There is no standard one-call Horizon/RPC operation that discovers every SAC and arbitrary SEP-41 token held by any address. Use complete token-event/indexer data to discover candidate contracts, then query current balance/metadata. Arbitrary SEP-41 storage layout is not standardized, so generic contract-data parsing is not universally valid. Return network, snapshot ledger/source, full asset/contract identity, decimals/units, and amount provenance.
keyFacts: Uses Horizon for G-account native/Classic balances and forbids counting a second SAC copy.
Uses token/SAC contract state for C-address or known-token balances.
States that all-token discovery requires an indexed token universe/events and current validation.
Does not assume arbitrary SEP-41 storage; reports identity, decimals, source, network, and snapshot.
avoid: Do NOT promise all token balances from one RPC/Horizon call or double-count a G trustline as a SAC copy.
Do NOT parse arbitrary SEP-41 storage as standardized or mix raw values without decimals/identity.
Do NOT imply Horizon account JSON lists C-address contract token state.

q-ti-find-export-secret-key.why: The public-key irreversibility and safety core was correct, but generic wallet wording underanswered current Freighter/CLI behavior and omitted imported-key, non-exportable-signer, lost-backup, and local-only handling boundaries.

corroboration.claim: A public G address cannot reveal its secret; current local CLI can export supported local identities, but secret material must never cross into chat/logs/forms.
classes: A: official keypair and CLI key documentation
B: current stellar-cli secret-command implementation
F: CLI 27 help inspected without invoking export

corroboration.claim: Current Freighter exposes a password-gated mnemonic recovery-phrase flow, while an imported raw key is not recoverable from that phrase; some signer classes are intentionally non-exportable.

rootCause: Eval-side product/safety incompleteness; CLI cookbook hardening and a Freighter export-support matrix remain research candidates.

golden.answer:
A public Stellar `G...` address and on-chain data **cannot be reversed** into its secret. Recovery/export is possible only from an existing wallet/CLI keystore, recovery phrase/backup, or custody provider—and only through that product's documented local flow.

Current Stellar CLI provides a local secret/recovery export for supported stored identities; use it only on the trusted local machine and never paste, echo, screenshot, log, commit, or enter the result into chat, tickets, web forms, or shell history. Current Freighter provides a password-gated **Show recovery phrase** flow for its mnemonic-derived wallet. Its source warns that an imported raw secret is not recoverable from that mnemonic; no current primary UI path for exporting that imported key was independently verified. Hardware wallets, smart accounts, KMS/HSM, and custodial signers may be intentionally non-exportable—use their signing/recovery process. If the backup is lost, move funds with another already-authorized signer if possible; otherwise do not invent recovery.
keyFacts: States that a public address cannot reveal a secret and secret material grants control.
Keeps any CLI/product export local and forbids requesting/displaying real secrets or phrases.
Scopes Freighter recovery phrase to mnemonic-derived accounts and does not invent an imported-key export menu.
Recognizes intentionally non-exportable signers and the lost-backup boundary.
avoid: Do NOT request, reveal, transform, validate, echo, generate, or store an actual secret key/recovery phrase.
Do NOT imply a G address is reversible or invent a Freighter imported-key export menu.
Do NOT advise reading plaintext browser/config storage or treat hardware/custodial/smart accounts as necessarily exportable.

classes: A: current Freighter connecting docs

golden.answer:
Use **`@stellar/freighter-api`** in a browser/client context after mount and inspect the structured `isConnected()` result/error. Separate installation detection from authorization: `isConnected` checks the extension, `isAllowed` checks allowed state, and `requestAccess` is the permission/public-address flow. Verify the same browser profile/window, extension enabled/unlocked, reload after install/update, network, site permission, and a top-level page rather than an iframe before investigating RPC/Horizon CORS.

Current Freighter 5.43 source/manifest matches `<all_urls>` at `document_start`, omits `all_frames`, and falls back after no content-script response; plain top-level HTTP localhost is therefore not categorically blocked by a Freighter origin allowlist. Official Stellar frontend docs nevertheless say HTTPS is required. Use HTTPS as the supported setup/workaround when needed, but do not grade it as the only possible detection fix or claim localhost universally works. Never bypass the wallet by exporting a secret into local code.
keyFacts: Uses supported client-side Freighter APIs and distinguishes installed, allowed/authorized, unlocked, and connected.
Troubleshoots profile/extension/reload/top-level frame/errors before chain CORS.
Preserves the official HTTPS versus current source/manifest disagreement instead of pinning a universal rule.
Keeps signing in the wallet and secrets out of local code.
avoid: Do NOT call isConnected during SSR or treat its structured result as a boolean/authorization state.
Do NOT claim localhost needs a Freighter allowlist entry, that HTTP always works, or that HTTPS always fixes detection.
Do NOT lead with CSP/RPC CORS without evidence or export a secret as a workaround.

golden.answer:
Public Testnet Friendbot is rate-limited, but no durable official requests-per-hour number was corroborated. On `429`, honor `Retry-After` when present or back off with jitter; do not rotate IPs or hammer retries. For many public-Testnet accounts, fund one account once and create/fund additional accounts with normal `CreateAccount`/payments.

For hermetic CI/bulk fixtures, run Quickstart in **local** mode and use its local Friendbot. Quickstart Testnet/Futurenet modes proxy the public faucets and do not evade their limits. As of **2026-07-11**, Validation Cloud advertises a Stellar Testnet XLM faucet, but access/limits are provider-specific and not assumed independent of Friendbot. Futurenet is a reset-prone different network, not a quota bypass; Circle's faucet supplies test **USDC**, not native XLM/base reserve. Never send credentials or Mainnet funds to obtain test funds, and do not trust an alleged official backup wallet without current primary documentation.

RPC Archive/data-lake integration currently extends **`getLedgers` only**. In the 2026-07-11 verification, a Quasar archive endpoint returned ledger 2 through getLedgers but rejected getEvents at ledger 2. For older events, continuously ingest and deduplicate by event ID, persist cursor/coverage only after downstream success, use a historical event index/Hubble, or replay complete ledger metadata through Galexie/Ingest SDK. Respect scan/filter/page limits and report gaps. CAP-67 (Final, Protocol 23) standardizes unified **asset** events, not arbitrary custom-event topics, and retroactive protocol events exist only when archives were re-ingested with compatible event-generation behavior.

corroboration.claim: Horizon/RPC account/ledger-entry reads are current-state; point-in-time Classic balances require latest-at-or-before state-change reconstruction per natural key with deletion/recreation handling.
classes: A: Hubble history/state, accounts/trustlines dictionaries, and Ingest ledger-reader docs
B: historical getLedgerEntries capability record/source boundary

golden.answer:
Current Horizon account reads and RPC `getLedgerEntries` do not provide an account-balance-at-ledger parameter. Resolve the requested timestamp to a **closed ledger**, then use Hubble account/trustline state-change history and select the latest row at or before that ledger for each natural key, honoring deleted/recreated accounts and trustlines plus the exact network/passphrase. Hubble state tables are change logs, not SCD2 snapshots; `batch_run_date` is a partition/load proxy, not exact state time, so rank by ledger/closed time.

For SAC/custom-token balances, reconstruct from contract-data history where token storage semantics are known, or replay complete ledger metadata/events and validate the token interface. History archives provide checkpoint state at 64-ledger boundaries; exact arbitrary-ledger state requires replaying changes from the preceding checkpoint. Report whether a result is exact or reconstructed and distinguish nominal balances from liabilities/reserves/spendable amounts. USD valuation is a separate timestamp-aligned join to an external historical price source with explicit asset/quote methodology.
keyFacts: Uses Hubble/ingestion latest-at-or-before reconstruction, not current Horizon/RPC reads.
Handles deletion/recreation, network identity, closed-ledger mapping, and non-SCD2/batch-date semantics.
Covers SAC/custom contract state or complete replay and checkpoint-to-target reconstruction.
Separates exact/reconstructed and nominal/spendable provenance from external historical USD pricing.
avoid: Do NOT pass a historical ledger to current account/getLedgerEntries APIs or call a ledger header an account snapshot.
Do NOT use batch_run_date as exact state time or ignore deletion/recreation/network resets.
Do NOT equate nominal XLM with spendable XLM or invent historical USD prices from ledger data.

golden.answer:
As of **2026-07-11**, use **`network.lightsail:stellar-sdk:4.0.0`**; Maven Central metadata and the 2026-07-04 GitHub release agree. Recheck the current Maven/release record rather than treating that number as timeless.

A fee bump wraps a **fully signed inner transaction** in a separately signed outer fee-bump envelope. With Java SDK 4.0.0, parse/build the signed inner `Transaction`, then call `FeeBumpTransaction.createWithBaseFee(feeSource, baseFee, inner)` or `createWithFee(...)`; the old `FeeBumpTransaction.Builder` is removed. The inner source still supplies sequence, time bounds, and required signatures. The fee-source account pays/signs the outer and needs the applicable low-threshold authorization/XLM; verify the total fee for the inner operation count plus wrapper and preserve Soroban resource fees.

For an external wallet, send only the XDR that wallet-controlled role should sign—usually inner XDR to the user wallet, then outer fee-bump XDR to the fee payer if it controls that account. Never export a wallet secret into Java. Submit/poll the final fee-bump envelope and handle inner failure separately.

golden.answer:
An `S...` value is SEP-23 StrKey encoding for one **32-byte Ed25519 secret seed**. It is not a BIP39 mnemonic and not BIP39's 64-byte derived seed. SEP-5 works forward: a BIP39 mnemonic plus the exact optional BIP39 passphrase produces a 64-byte seed; SLIP-10 then derives hardened Stellar children at `m/44'/148'/x'`, with index 0 as the primary account, and the child seed is encoded as `S...`. Ed25519 SLIP-10 uses hardened private-parent derivation only.

There is no standardized inverse from one child `S...` to its original mnemonic, passphrase, master seed/chain code, path, or index. Encoding those 32 bytes as BIP39 entropy invents a new mnemonic and normally derives a different hierarchy. Recovery must use the exact phrase, optional passphrase, path and index; verify only derived public `G...` addresses offline. Direct `S...` import, account scanning and whether an imported key is covered by the wallet's mnemonic backup are wallet/version-specific.

**SEP-52 is not a 24-word encoding of one secret key.** Current SEP-52 v0.1.0 is **Draft** “Key Sharing Method for Stellar Keys”: it extends SEP-5 with SLIP-39 Shamir mnemonic shares. The shares are not BIP39 phrases; the proposal recommends 256-bit entropy, which produces 33-word SLIP-39 shares in the reference behavior.

As of **2026-07-11**, `stellar keys generate <name>` creates a new 24-word phrase; `--seed` is deterministic generation input, not the normal phrase-import flag. Current import is prompted `stellar keys add --secure-store`; deprecated `--secret-key`/`--seed-phrase` inputs also prompt, and `--hd-path` chooses the derived account. Never put a mnemonic or S-key in argv. Lab Saved Keypairs is documented for Testnet/Futurenet only, never Mainnet; current Laboratory source serializes saved keypair objects, including secret values in tests, as plaintext JSON in browser `localStorage`. Treat that as throwaway-test convenience, not secure or offline custody.
keyFacts: Distinguishes one 32-byte S-key from a BIP39 mnemonic and 64-byte BIP39 seed.
Explains exact SEP-5/BIP39/SLIP-10 forward derivation at hardened m/44'/148'/x', including passphrase and index.
States no standardized inverse exists and raw-byte-to-words encoding creates a new hierarchy.
Defines SEP-52 as Draft SLIP-39 threshold sharing extending SEP-5, with non-BIP39 33-word shares for the recommended 256-bit entropy—not a 24-word one-key representation.
Uses current prompted CLI generate/import/secure-store/HD-path semantics and forbids argv secrets.
Treats Lab Saved Keypairs as Testnet/Futurenet-only plaintext browser storage, and keeps direct-import backup behavior wallet/version-specific.
avoid: Do NOT say a public G-address can reveal a secret or that an S-key can recover its original mnemonic/passphrase/path/index.
Do NOT conflate an S-key, BIP39 entropy/seed, SEP-5 hierarchy, SEP-23 StrKey or SEP-52/SLIP-39 shares.
Do NOT call SEP-52 a Final 24-word representation of one S-key or call SLIP-39 shares interchangeable with BIP39.
Do NOT omit hardened apostrophes or invent non-hardened change/address path components for SEP-5.
Do NOT present --seed as mnemonic import, put secrets in argv/environment/logs, or use an online converter.
Do NOT assume a direct import joins or is backed up by a wallet mnemonic, or store production secrets in Lab/localStorage.

golden.answer:
Self-hosting does not automatically provide complete queryable history. Separate the components:

- **Stellar Core** verifies/maintains current ledger state and BucketList state. A complete history archive plus `CATCHUP_COMPLETE=true` can replay all available history, but that may take weeks and does not create an indexed REST/JSON-RPC history API. Core's optional query server is disabled by default and, when enabled, keeps a small best-effort set of recent state snapshots rather than arbitrary history.
- A newly initialized publishing **history archive** normally starts forward; old checkpoints require separate mirroring/repair. **Captive Core** streams historical/live ledgers to an ingester but is not durable storage.
- **Stellar RPC** `getTransaction`, `getTransactions` and `getEvents` read retained local rows. Its direct external-datastore “Infinite Scroll” path is currently wired only to **`getLedgers`**. RPC v27.1.1 may validate/fetch an older datastore range while response `oldestLedger` still reflects the local database, so do not infer datastore depth from that field alone.
- **Horizon** can serve full indexed classic history only after explicit historical ingestion plus sufficient retention, storage and filter coverage. Current account state or the latest ledger proves nothing about older row coverage.
- **Galexie** exports raw `LedgerCloseMeta` to a data lake; it is storage/transport, not a query API. **Hubble** is read-only batch BigQuery analytics, not live transaction serving. Normal replayable close metadata begins at ledger 2 because ledger 1 is pregenerated genesis state.

As of **2026-07-11**, those boundaries were checked against Core 27.1.0, RPC 27.1.1, Horizon 27.0.0 and Galexie 27.0.0. Exact coverage of an unnamed deployment is unverifiable without its local database, archive/data-lake completeness, filters and active configuration. For a product, choose the method and latency model first, then inspect current provider capabilities or run the corresponding self-hosted ingestion path.
keyFacts: Separates Core replay/current state and raw history archives from indexed query services.
States complete catchup can be slow and Core query snapshots are recent/current-state oriented, not arbitrary history.
Treats a new archive as forward-only unless old checkpoints are mirrored and Captive Core as transport, not storage.
Keeps RPC transaction/event methods local-retention-bound and direct datastore history getLedgers-only, including the local oldestLedger caveat.
Requires explicit retained Horizon ingestion and distinguishes Galexie raw export from Hubble batch read-only analytics.
States ordinary replayable LedgerCloseMeta starts at ledger 2 and keeps deployment/provider coverage dated and inspectable.
avoid: Do NOT call Core, a history archive, Captive Core or Galexie an indexed history API.
Do NOT equate complete replay, raw archive completeness, local retention, indexed ingestion and analytics.
Do NOT claim pointing RPC at a synced Core backfills arbitrary old getEvents/getTransactions or that a datastore extends every method.
Do NOT infer datastore floor from v27.1.1 oldestLedger or Horizon coverage from current state/latest ledger.
Do NOT call Hubble real-time/free or Galexie a query API, and do not require a literal replayable ledger-1 record.
Do NOT publish an undated static provider list or exact deployment coverage without inspecting live configuration/data.

golden.answer:
Use the current Stellar Lab at `lab.stellar.org` for Mainnet/Testnet/Futurenet selection; transaction build, sign, simulate and submit; contract deploy/invoke; RPC/Horizon exploration; XDR↔JSON; and saved/shareable requests. A current transaction walkthrough may say: select the network and source, **Fetch next sequence**, add `Change Trust` or another operation, then Build/Sign/Submit—but do not freeze screen coordinates or assume one wallet.

Current signing paths include an extension wallet through the wallet kit, Ledger/hash-with-Ledger, Trezor, external signatures, and raw secret input. Prefer wallet, hardware or a separately trusted external signer. Lab's current source performs raw-secret signing in browser JavaScript. Saved Keypairs is documented for **Testnet/Futurenet only, never Mainnet**; current master serializes saved keypair objects to browser `localStorage` as plaintext JSON, and tests persist the `S...` value directly. Do not treat Saved as encrypted custody.

Lab can be self-hosted, and Quickstart exposes a local `/lab` with custom endpoints, but local/self-hosted is not automatically offline or air-gapped. Hosted APIs, RPC/Horizon calls, simulation/submission and package/runtime loading use a network unless the environment is deliberately isolated and inspected. Source review does not prove a universal 'never transmits secrets' negative. For a real air gap, export unsigned XDR to a separately trusted offline/hardware signer and import only the signature/signed envelope.

For SACs, `stellar contract asset deploy` deploys the Stellar Asset Contract; `stellar contract id asset` only derives/returns the deterministic C-address. Do not claim Lab has a dedicated SAC-creation flow unless the current UI is reverified. Friendbot remains non-production Testnet funding, and a trustline does not itself credit asset units.

Distinguishes stellar contract asset deploy from contract id asset deterministic lookup.
Keeps Friendbot and trustline behavior network/asset-specific.
avoid: Do NOT call Saved Keypairs encrypted, secure, offline, air-gapped or appropriate for a Mainnet seed.
Do NOT assert hosted/self-hosted Lab universally never transmits secrets or require Freighter as the only signer.
Do NOT paste/save production secrets, sign/submit during read-only verification, or treat Quickstart as production infrastructure.
Do NOT claim contract id asset deploys a SAC or invent a dedicated current Lab SAC button without rechecking.
Do NOT freeze old Laboratory URLs/screenshots/coordinates, use Mainnet Friendbot, or say a trustline credits tokens.

golden.answer:
Testnet and Mainnet are independent ledgers with different network passphrases/network IDs, endpoints, histories and state. Nothing is promoted on-chain. Recreate/fund accounts and XLM, reissue and distribute assets, recreate trustlines/offers/authorization, redeploy and initialize contracts/storage, and replace every network-specific contract ID, SAC C-address, endpoint, passphrase, allowlist and indexer/explorer record. Initialize Core/Horizon/RPC databases for the selected network; do not switch or copy a Testnet database into Mainnet. Deliberately seed only the off-chain application data production actually needs.

The same Ed25519 keypair can produce the same `G...` text on both networks, but the accounts and balances remain separate. Reusing a test seed on Mainnet is a security/operational choice, not state migration. Fresh production keys are prudent—and explicitly required for some privileged distribution/treasury roles such as documented SDP setups—but are not a Stellar protocol law for every address.

For many user accounts, submit ordinary funded `CreateAccount` operations under current reserve, operation, fee, sequence and retry constraints. Channel accounts are optional transaction-source sequence lanes for throughput; they are not an account-creation primitive or migration requirement. SDP is a managed disbursement platform, not a faucet. Mainnet has no Friendbot, and Testnet can reset. Never reuse Testnet transaction XDR/signatures, contract IDs/SACs, issuer configuration or persisted chain history as if it were production state.

Forbids Mainnet Friendbot and Testnet XDR/signature/database reuse.

Do NOT require new keys for every address as protocol law or casually reuse a test secret for privileged Mainnet authority.
Do NOT call channel accounts an account-creation primitive/requirement or SDP a faucet.
Do NOT reuse Testnet XDR/signatures/endpoints/passphrase/contract IDs/SACs or switch a Testnet database to Mainnet.
Do NOT assume issuer/distribution/authorization or off-chain/indexer state appears automatically on Mainnet.

golden.answer:
Friendbot creates/funds a Testnet account with fake **XLM only**; it is not a USDC faucet. First ensure the Testnet account exists and has enough XLM for its base reserve, then establish a trustline to the current Circle-issued Testnet USDC asset. A trustline only permits holding the asset—it does not credit any USDC.

As of **2026-07-11**, Circle Faucet supported Stellar Testnet and the classic USDC issuer was `GBBD47IF6LWK7P7MDEVSCWR7DPUWV3NY3DTQEVFL4NAT4AQH3ZLLFLA5`. The UI observation of 20 test USDC per address/chain per two hours is volatile and is not a grading requirement; recheck issuer, chain support, amount and cooldown before use. A Circle faucet payment is separate from Friendbot, and an SDF/Lab path-payment demo is another distinct distribution path.

For classic accounts, identify USDC by code plus issuer/trustline. Its deterministic SAC C-address is the contract bridge for the same classic asset, not a second issuer or a wrapped token. Use only Testnet endpoints/assets and never represent faucet units as redeemable Mainnet value. Maintenance verification should inspect public issuer/service data without calling a faucet.

q-ti-tx-too-late-resubmit.why: The inherited core separated tx_too_late from tx_bad_seq, but it did not precisely scope fee/sequence effects to an ordinary outer precondition rejection, blurred hash lookup with resubmission evidence, and implied fee bump/higher fee was a generic expiry remedy.

corroboration.claim: txTOO_LATE means ledger close time exceeded maxTime; an ordinary outer transaction rejected during precondition validation is not applied, increments no sequence and charges no on-chain fee
classes: A: official result-code and transaction-lifecycle documentation
B: Stellar Core TransactionFrame precondition/apply implementation
F: published/local lifecycle fixtures without live submission

corroboration.claim: Ambiguous submission requires old-hash/account polling; after confirmed absence and expiry, rebuild current sequence/bounds/fee, re-simulate Soroban, re-sign and track a new hash
classes: A: official error-handling/lifecycle guidance
B: signature-base and sequence implementation semantics

evidence: Solo scratchpad 575, GT-54 primary 3383 and blind 3386 independently retained the duplicate/hash guard; blind helper 3388 supplied the exact fee/sequence/failure-stage scope before seal.

Sibling sweep checked classic submission errors, sequence ordering, finality, fee bump/channel accounts and Soroban simulation; no blanket no-fee/no-sequence rule is applied to fee-bump inner results or post-inclusion failures.
rootCause: Eval-side lifecycle precision gap: transaction hash lookup, unchanged-envelope resubmission, precondition expiry, sequence/fee consumption and fee-bump/post-inclusion stages were treated as one failure state.

golden.answer:
Protocol `txTOO_LATE` (Horizon `tx_too_late`) means the ledger close time is later than the transaction's `maxTime`; `maxTime=0` has no upper bound. It is distinct from `txBAD_SEQ`. For an ordinary outer transaction rejected during precondition validation, expiry itself does not apply the transaction, increment the source sequence, or charge an on-chain fee. A concurrent or successful transaction may still have changed the account sequence, and fee-bump inner/outer or post-inclusion failures must be analyzed separately.

If the original submission outcome is ambiguous, query the old transaction hash and current account state until the transaction is found or the time bound has expired. A lookup miss before expiry is not proof it was never submitted. If it is found/included, do not rebuild the same intent. If it remains absent after expiry, resubmitting the unchanged envelope will still be too late: reload the current next sequence, choose appropriate future bounds and current fee, re-simulate Soroban transactions/authorization/footprint, rebuild, re-sign, submit, and track the **new hash**.

Do not edit signed XDR and retain the old signatures—bounds, sequence, fee and Soroban data are signed contents, so any change creates a new signature base. Preserve idempotency/intent records so a timeout cannot turn into a duplicate payment.
keyFacts: Defines txTOO_LATE/maxTime=0 and distinguishes txBAD_SEQ.
Scopes no sequence increment/no on-chain fee to an ordinary outer precondition rejection and keeps concurrent/fee-bump/post-inclusion cases separate.
Uses old-hash and account-state polling for ambiguous submission and does not treat an early lookup miss as final.
After confirmed absence/expiry, reloads current sequence, rebuilds future bounds/current fee, re-simulates Soroban, re-signs and tracks a new hash.
Forbids editing XDR while retaining signatures and requires duplicate-intent/idempotency protection.
avoid: Do NOT simply resubmit an expired signed envelope or call tx_too_late tx_bad_seq.
Do NOT claim every failed transaction preserves sequence/fee or apply the ordinary precondition rule to all fee-bump/post-inclusion outcomes.
Do NOT treat a hash lookup miss before expiry as proof of rejection or rebuild when the old transaction was found.
Do NOT reuse a stale sequence, manually alter XDR with old signatures, or skip Soroban re-simulation.
Do NOT present higher fee/fee bump as the fix for an expired maxTime or create duplicate intent after a timeout.

Golden cites: Transaction result code docs for `tx_too_late` and `tx_bad_seq`. | Error-handling/time-bounds docs for safe resubmission guidance.
